questions.stem: A 58-year-old man presents to the emergency department with chest pain. A 12-lead ECG is obtained. The physician notices that lead II shows the tallest P waves and the most upright QRS complexes compared to other limb leads. Which of the following best explains this finding?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Lead II is most parallel to the normal mean electrical axis

choices.label: B
choices.text: Lead II is an augmented unipolar limb lead with amplified voltage

choices.label: C
choices.text: Lead II measures the potential difference between the right and left arms

choices.label: D
choices.text: Lead II has the shortest interelectrode distance of all limb leads

choices.label: E
choices.text: Lead II is perpendicular to the mean electrical axis

explanation.summary: Lead II records the potential difference between the right arm (negative) and left leg (positive), creating a vector at approximately +60 degrees. Because the normal mean electrical axis of the heart is approximately +60 degrees, lead II is most parallel to this axis and therefore records the largest deflections.
explanation.whyCorrect: The normal mean electrical axis is approximately +60 degrees, which aligns closely with the orientation of lead II (+60 degrees). When a lead is parallel to the electrical axis, it records the maximum voltage deflection.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Lead II is a standard bipolar limb lead (Einthoven), not an augmented unipolar lead. Augmented leads are aVR, aVL, and aVF.
whyWrongByChoice.C: This describes lead I, which measures the potential between the right arm (negative) and left arm (positive).
whyWrongByChoice.D: Interelectrode distance does not determine deflection amplitude in standard ECG lead configurations; the relationship to the electrical axis does.
whyWrongByChoice.E: If lead II were perpendicular to the axis, it would show the smallest (isoelectric) deflections, not the tallest.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman undergoes a routine 12-lead ECG. The technician accidentally places the right arm and left arm electrodes in reversed positions. Which of the following ECG changes would be expected as a result of this error?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Tall R waves in the right precordial leads

choices.label: B
choices.text: Inverted P waves and QRS complexes in lead I

choices.label: C
choices.text: ST elevation in leads II, III, and aVF

choices.label: D
choices.text: Low-voltage QRS complexes in all leads

choices.label: E
choices.text: Prolonged QT interval across all 12 leads

explanation.summary: Reversing the right and left arm electrodes inverts the polarity of lead I. Since lead I normally records a positive P wave and upright QRS (with the positive electrode on the left arm), switching the electrodes causes these waveforms to become inverted in lead I.
explanation.whyCorrect: Lead I measures the potential difference from right arm (−) to left arm (+). Reversing these electrodes flips the polarity, causing all normally upright waveforms in lead I to become inverted.

whyWrongByChoice.C: ST elevation in inferior leads suggests myocardial injury, not lead misplacement. Limb lead reversal would not produce ST elevation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Low-voltage QRS complexes suggest pericardial effusion, obesity, or COPD, not electrode reversal.
whyWrongByChoice.E: QT prolongation is caused by electrolyte abnormalities or medications, not electrode misplacement.
whyWrongByChoice.A: Lead II is a standard bipolar limb lead (Einthoven), not an augmented unipolar lead. Augmented leads are aVR, aVL, and aVF.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old man with acute chest pain has an ECG showing ST-segment elevation in leads V1 through V4. The physician orders additional right-sided precordial leads. Which of the following anatomic regions is best evaluated by right-sided precordial leads?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Lateral wall of the left ventricle

choices.label: B
choices.text: Posterior wall of the left ventricle

choices.label: C
choices.text: Right ventricular free wall

choices.label: D
choices.text: Interventricular septum

choices.label: E
choices.text: Left atrial appendage

explanation.summary: Right-sided precordial leads (V3R-V6R) are placed in mirror-image positions on the right side of the chest and are used to detect right ventricular infarction, which often accompanies inferior STEMI.
explanation.whyCorrect: Right-sided leads are specifically positioned to overlie the right ventricle, making them the best tool for detecting right ventricular ischemia or infarction, particularly ST elevation in V4R.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The lateral wall is assessed by leads I, aVL, V5, and V6, not right-sided leads.
whyWrongByChoice.B: The posterior wall is assessed by posterior leads (V7-V9) or reciprocal changes in V1-V3, not right-sided leads.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The interventricular septum is evaluated by leads V1-V2 in the standard configuration.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The left atrial appendage is not specifically evaluated by any standard or supplemental ECG lead configuration.

questions.stem: A medical student is learning about Einthoven's triangle. She is told that the voltage measured in lead II equals the sum of the voltages in leads I and III. A patient's ECG shows a lead I voltage of +0.4 mV and a lead III voltage of +0.8 mV. Which of the following is the expected voltage in lead II according to Einthoven's law?

explanation.summary: Einthoven's law states that Lead II = Lead I + Lead III. With Lead I = +0.4 mV and Lead III = +0.8 mV, Lead II = 0.4 + 0.8 = +1.2 mV.
explanation.whyCorrect: Applying Einthoven's equation directly: Lead II = Lead I + Lead III = 0.4 + 0.8 = 1.2 mV.

whyWrongByChoice.A: This is the value of lead I alone and does not account for the addition of lead III.
whyWrongByChoice.B: This is the value of lead III alone and does not account for the addition of lead I.
whyWrongByChoice.E: This value has no mathematical basis from the given lead voltages using Einthoven's equation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: This would result from doubling lead III, which does not follow Einthoven's law.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man presents with dizziness. His ECG shows an equiphasic (equally positive and negative) QRS complex in lead I and a maximally positive QRS in lead aVF. Which of the following best describes his mean electrical axis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: −30 degrees (left axis deviation)

choices.label: B
choices.text: 0 degrees (normal axis)

choices.label: C
choices.text: +180 degrees (extreme axis deviation)

choices.label: D
choices.text: +120 degrees (marked right axis deviation)

choices.label: E
choices.text: +90 degrees (right axis deviation)

explanation.summary: An equiphasic QRS in lead I means the electrical axis is perpendicular to lead I (0 degrees), which is either +90 or −90 degrees. A maximally positive QRS in aVF (+90 degrees) confirms the axis is directed toward +90 degrees.
explanation.whyCorrect: When a lead shows an equiphasic complex, the axis is perpendicular to that lead. Lead I is at 0 degrees, so the axis is at +90 or −90. The positive aVF confirms +90 degrees.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Left axis deviation at −30 degrees would produce a positive QRS in lead I, not an equiphasic one.
whyWrongByChoice.B: An axis of 0 degrees would produce a maximally positive QRS in lead I, not an equiphasic complex.
whyWrongByChoice.D: +120 degrees would produce a negative QRS in lead I (not equiphasic) and a positive but not maximal deflection in aVF.
whyWrongByChoice.C: +180 degrees would produce a negative QRS in lead I and an equiphasic QRS in aVF.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman has an ECG performed with standard calibration. Each small box on the ECG paper represents 1 mm. At standard speed and voltage settings, which of the following correctly describes the time and voltage represented by one small box?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 0.04 seconds and 0.1 mV

choices.label: B
choices.text: 0.02 seconds and 0.05 mV

choices.label: C
choices.text: 0.04 seconds and 0.5 mV

choices.label: D
choices.text: 0.02 seconds and 0.5 mV

choices.label: E
choices.text: 0.1 seconds and 0.1 mV

explanation.summary: At standard ECG calibration (25 mm/sec paper speed and 10 mm/mV gain), each small box (1 mm) represents 0.04 seconds in time and 0.1 mV in voltage.
explanation.whyCorrect: At 25 mm/sec, 1 mm = 1/25 sec = 0.04 sec. At 10 mm/mV standard gain, 1 mm = 1/10 mV = 0.1 mV.

whyWrongByChoice.C: While 0.04 seconds is correct for the time axis, 0.5 mV per small box would require a gain of only 2 mm/mV, which is far below standard.
whyWrongByChoice.D: 0.02 seconds corresponds to 50 mm/sec paper speed, and 0.5 mV per mm is non-standard gain.
whyWrongByChoice.E: 0.1 seconds per small box would correspond to a paper speed of 10 mm/sec, which is non-standard.
whyWrongByChoice.B: 0.02 seconds would correspond to a paper speed of 50 mm/sec, not the standard 25 mm/sec, and 0.05 mV would require a 20 mm/mV gain setting.

questions.stem: A 48-year-old man is undergoing cardiac evaluation. His ECG shows a negative QRS complex in both lead I and lead aVF. Which of the following best describes the quadrant in which the mean electrical axis lies?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Normal axis (0 to +90 degrees)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Extreme right axis deviation (−90 to −180 degrees)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Right axis deviation (+90 to +180 degrees)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Left axis deviation (−30 to −90 degrees)

choices.label: E
choices.text: Physiologic axis (−30 to 0 degrees)

explanation.summary: A negative QRS in lead I indicates the axis points away from 0 degrees (toward the right). A negative QRS in aVF indicates the axis points away from +90 degrees (upward). The only quadrant that is both rightward and upward is the northwest quadrant (−90 to ±180 degrees), termed extreme axis deviation.
explanation.whyCorrect: Negative QRS in lead I means the axis is beyond +90 or −90 (rightward). Negative QRS in aVF means the axis is above 0 (upward). The intersection is −90 to ±180 degrees, or extreme/northwest axis.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Normal axis would produce positive QRS in both lead I and aVF.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Right axis deviation (+90 to +180) would show a negative QRS in lead I but a positive QRS in aVF.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A physiologic axis near 0 degrees would produce a strongly positive QRS in lead I.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Left axis deviation would show a positive QRS in lead I but negative in aVF (for angles more negative than −30).

questions.stem: During a 12-lead ECG, a technician places the V1 electrode. Which of the following is the correct anatomic landmark for V1 placement?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Left sternal border, fourth intercostal space

choices.label: B
choices.text: Right sternal border, second intercostal space

choices.label: C
choices.text: Right sternal border, fourth intercostal space

choices.label: D
choices.text: Left midclavicular line, fifth intercostal space

choices.label: E
choices.text: Left anterior axillary line, fifth intercostal space

explanation.summary: V1 is placed at the right sternal border at the fourth intercostal space. V2 is placed at the left sternal border at the fourth intercostal space.
explanation.whyCorrect: The standard placement for V1 is the fourth intercostal space at the right sternal border. This position allows assessment of septal electrical activity.

whyWrongByChoice.A: This describes the placement for V2, not V1.
whyWrongByChoice.D: This describes the placement for V4 (left midclavicular line, fifth intercostal space).
whyWrongByChoice.E: This describes the placement for V5 (left anterior axillary line, fifth intercostal space).
whyWrongByChoice.B: The second intercostal space is used as a landmark to count down to the fourth intercostal space, but no precordial lead is placed there.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man with COPD has an ECG showing low-voltage QRS complexes, right axis deviation, and P pulmonale. The physician notes that lead aVR shows a predominantly positive QRS complex. Which of the following is the orientation of lead aVR?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 0 degrees, pointing toward the left arm

choices.label: B
choices.text: +60 degrees, pointing toward the left leg

choices.label: C
choices.text: +90 degrees, pointing inferiorly

choices.label: D
choices.text: −150 degrees, pointing toward the right arm

choices.label: E
choices.text: −30 degrees, pointing toward the left shoulder

explanation.summary: Lead aVR is an augmented unipolar lead with its positive electrode on the right arm. Its orientation is −150 degrees (or equivalently, it points toward the right shoulder, away from the heart's normal electrical activity).
explanation.whyCorrect: aVR has its positive pole directed toward the right arm at −150 degrees. Normally, QRS is negative in aVR because the heart's electrical activity moves away from the right arm. A positive QRS in aVR is seen with extreme axis deviation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: 0 degrees describes the orientation of lead I, not aVR.
whyWrongByChoice.B: +60 degrees describes the orientation of lead II.
whyWrongByChoice.C: +90 degrees describes the orientation of lead aVF.
whyWrongByChoice.E: −30 degrees describes the orientation of lead aVL.

questions.stem: A 34-year-old woman undergoes ECG monitoring. The ECG machine uses a central terminal (Wilson's central terminal) as a reference for the precordial leads. Which of the following best describes how Wilson's central terminal is constructed?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Amplifying the signal from the electrode closest to the cardiac apex

choices.label: B
choices.text: Using the potential from the right leg electrode as ground reference

choices.label: C
choices.text: Measuring the potential difference between adjacent precordial electrodes

choices.label: D
choices.text: Connecting the V1 and V6 electrodes to create a bipolar chest lead

choices.label: E
choices.text: Averaging the potentials from the right arm, left arm, and left leg electrodes

explanation.summary: Wilson's central terminal is created by connecting the right arm, left arm, and left leg electrodes through equal resistances to form a single reference point that approximates zero potential, used as the negative pole for all unipolar precordial leads.
explanation.whyCorrect: Wilson's central terminal averages the potentials from three limb electrodes (RA, LA, LL) through equal resistors, creating a relatively stable near-zero reference against which each precordial electrode measures voltage.

whyWrongByChoice.B: The right leg electrode serves as the ground (to reduce electrical noise) but is not part of Wilson's central terminal.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Precordial leads are unipolar, not bipolar. They each measure voltage relative to Wilson's central terminal, not relative to each other.
whyWrongByChoice.D: V1 and V6 are independent unipolar leads, not connected to form a bipolar lead.
whyWrongByChoice.A: Augmented limb leads (aVR, aVL, aVF) use amplification, but Wilson's central terminal is a passive averaging circuit.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man presents with acute onset substernal chest pain. His ECG shows ST elevation in leads I, aVL, V5, and V6. Which of the following coronary artery territories is most likely affected?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Left circumflex artery, lateral territory

choices.label: B
choices.text: Left anterior descending, anterior territory

choices.label: C
choices.text: Right coronary artery, inferior territory

choices.label: D
choices.text: Posterior descending artery, posterior territory

choices.label: E
choices.text: Left main coronary artery, global territory

explanation.summary: ST elevation in leads I, aVL, V5, and V6 corresponds to the lateral wall of the left ventricle, which is supplied by the left circumflex artery.
explanation.whyCorrect: Leads I, aVL (high lateral) and V5, V6 (low lateral) reflect the lateral wall, which is supplied by the left circumflex artery or its obtuse marginal branches.

whyWrongByChoice.B: The anterior territory (LAD) would produce ST elevation in leads V1-V4.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The posterior territory would show ST depression in V1-V3 as reciprocal changes, or ST elevation in posterior leads V7-V9.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Left main occlusion would cause widespread ST changes, often with ST elevation in aVR and diffuse ST depression.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The inferior territory (RCA) would produce ST elevation in leads II, III, and aVF.

questions.stem: A physician explains to a medical student that precordial leads V1-V6 show a progressive change in QRS morphology from a predominantly negative complex in V1 to a predominantly positive complex in V6. Which of the following terms best describes the lead where the R wave and S wave are approximately equal in amplitude?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Augmentation point

choices.label: B
choices.text: Transitional zone

choices.label: C
choices.text: Electrical fulcrum

choices.label: D
choices.text: Null vector lead

choices.label: E
choices.text: Isoelectric reference

explanation.summary: The transitional zone (or transition point) is the precordial lead where the R wave and S wave amplitudes are approximately equal. Normally this occurs around V3-V4.
explanation.whyCorrect: In the normal precordial R-wave progression, V1 has a small R and deep S, while V6 has a tall R and small S. The transition zone is where R equals S, typically at V3 or V4.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Augmentation refers to the process used in aVR, aVL, and aVF leads, not to precordial R-wave progression.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Electrical fulcrum is not a standard ECG term used in clinical practice.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Null vector lead is not standard terminology in electrocardiography.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Isoelectric reference describes the baseline of the ECG tracing, not the point of R-S wave equality.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old woman with a history of hypertension presents for evaluation. Her ECG shows a P wave duration of 140 ms with a notched appearance in lead II and a biphasic P wave in V1 with a prominent terminal negative deflection. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Right atrial enlargement

choices.label: B
choices.text: Biatrial enlargement

choices.label: C
choices.text: Left atrial enlargement

choices.label: D
choices.text: First-degree AV block

choices.label: E
choices.text: Junctional rhythm with retrograde P waves

explanation.summary: Left atrial enlargement (LAE) produces a broad (>120 ms), notched P wave in lead II (P mitrale) and a biphasic P wave in V1 with a deep, wide terminal negative component (>1 mm deep and >40 ms wide).
explanation.whyCorrect: The broad, notched P wave in lead II and prominent terminal negative deflection in V1 are classic criteria for left atrial enlargement. The prolonged P wave duration reflects delayed left atrial depolarization.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Right atrial enlargement produces tall, peaked P waves (>2.5 mm) in lead II (P pulmonale), not broad notched P waves.
whyWrongByChoice.D: First-degree AV block is defined by a PR interval >200 ms, which is not described here. P wave morphology changes are separate from PR interval prolongation.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Retrograde P waves are inverted in inferior leads and have a short RP interval, which does not match the described morphology.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Biatrial enlargement shows features of both RAE and LAE: tall and broad P waves. This case lacks the tall, peaked component of RAE.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old man has an ECG showing a PR interval of 240 ms that remains constant across all beats, with each P wave followed by a QRS complex. Which of the following best describes this conduction abnormality?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Mobitz type I second-degree AV block

choices.label: B
choices.text: Mobitz type II second-degree AV block

choices.label: C
choices.text: Third-degree (complete) AV block

choices.label: D
choices.text: First-degree AV block

choices.label: E
choices.text: Normal PR interval variant

explanation.summary: A PR interval >200 ms (here 240 ms) that is constant with 1:1 AV conduction (each P wave followed by a QRS) defines first-degree AV block. It represents delayed but not failed conduction through the AV node.
explanation.whyCorrect: First-degree AV block is diagnosed when the PR interval exceeds 200 ms and every P wave is conducted to the ventricles. The constant PR of 240 ms with 1:1 conduction is diagnostic.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Mobitz type I shows progressive PR prolongation before a dropped QRS. The constant PR interval here rules this out.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Mobitz type II shows constant PR intervals but with intermittently dropped QRS complexes. Here, no QRS complexes are dropped.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The normal PR interval is 120-200 ms. A PR of 240 ms exceeds the upper limit and is not a normal variant.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Third-degree AV block shows complete AV dissociation with independent P wave and QRS rates. Here, every P wave is conducted.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old man presents with palpitations. His ECG shows a short PR interval (<120 ms), a slurred upstroke of the QRS complex (delta wave), and a widened QRS duration of 140 ms. Which of the following is the underlying mechanism of these findings?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Delayed conduction through the His-Purkinje system

choices.label: B
choices.text: Accelerated conduction through the normal AV node

choices.label: C
choices.text: Enhanced automaticity of a ventricular focus

choices.label: D
choices.text: Retrograde conduction through the AV node

choices.label: E
choices.text: Early ventricular activation via an accessory bypass tract

explanation.summary: The triad of short PR interval, delta wave, and widened QRS is characteristic of Wolff-Parkinson-White (WPW) syndrome, caused by an accessory pathway (bundle of Kent) that allows premature ventricular activation bypassing the AV node.
explanation.whyCorrect: An accessory pathway conducts atrial impulses directly to the ventricle without the normal AV nodal delay, producing a short PR interval. Early ventricular activation via this tract creates the delta wave and widens the QRS.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Delayed His-Purkinje conduction would cause QRS widening (as in bundle branch block) but would not produce a short PR interval or delta wave.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Enhanced ventricular automaticity would produce ventricular premature complexes or ventricular tachycardia, not the consistent short PR with delta waves.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Retrograde AV nodal conduction produces inverted P waves after QRS complexes, not delta waves or short PR intervals.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Enhanced AV nodal conduction could shorten the PR interval but would not produce a delta wave or QRS widening.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old woman taking sotalol presents with syncope. Her ECG shows a heart rate of 55 bpm and a QTc interval of 580 ms. While on the monitor, she develops polymorphic ventricular tachycardia with undulating QRS axis. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial treatment?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Intravenous magnesium sulfate

choices.label: B
choices.text: Intravenous amiodarone bolus

choices.label: C
choices.text: Oral mexiletine loading dose

choices.label: D
choices.text: Intravenous procainamide infusion

choices.label: E
choices.text: Intravenous verapamil bolus

explanation.summary: This patient has torsades de pointes (TdP), a polymorphic ventricular tachycardia associated with prolonged QT interval. The first-line treatment is intravenous magnesium sulfate, regardless of serum magnesium levels.
explanation.whyCorrect: IV magnesium sulfate is the first-line treatment for torsades de pointes. It stabilizes the cardiac membrane and suppresses early afterdepolarizations that trigger TdP, even in patients with normal magnesium levels.

whyWrongByChoice.C: While mexiletine (a class IB agent that shortens QT) may be used adjunctively, oral administration is too slow for acute treatment of active TdP.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Procainamide is a class IA antiarrhythmic that prolongs the QT interval and would worsen torsades de pointes.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Verapamil does not treat the underlying mechanism of TdP and is not effective for this arrhythmia.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Amiodarone itself prolongs the QT interval and could worsen torsades de pointes. It is contraindicated in this setting.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with chest pain has an ECG showing an ST segment that is elevated and concave upward in leads II, III, aVF, and V4-V6, with PR segment depression in the same leads. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Acute anterior STEMI

choices.label: B
choices.text: Acute pericarditis

choices.label: C
choices.text: Prinzmetal angina

choices.label: D
choices.text: Left ventricular aneurysm

choices.label: E
choices.text: Brugada syndrome

explanation.summary: Diffuse concave-upward ST elevation with PR depression across multiple vascular territories is the hallmark of acute pericarditis. Unlike STEMI, pericarditis does not follow a single coronary distribution and shows PR depression.
explanation.whyCorrect: Acute pericarditis classically shows diffuse, concave-upward ST elevation not confined to a single coronary territory, with reciprocal PR segment depression. This pattern is distinct from STEMI's convex ST elevation in a territorial distribution.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Anterior STEMI would show ST elevation in V1-V4 with reciprocal ST depression in inferior leads. Diffuse elevation across multiple territories and PR depression favor pericarditis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Prinzmetal angina produces transient ST elevation in a single coronary territory during vasospasm, not diffuse changes with PR depression.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Left ventricular aneurysm produces persistent ST elevation (usually anterior) without acute evolution, not diffuse elevation with PR depression.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Brugada syndrome shows coved ST elevation in V1-V3 with a specific morphology, not diffuse changes with PR depression.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old man has an ECG showing a QRS duration of 160 ms with an rsR' pattern in V1 and wide, slurred S waves in leads I and V6. Which of the following is the most likely conduction abnormality?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Left bundle branch block

choices.label: B
choices.text: Left anterior fascicular block

choices.label: C
choices.text: Right bundle branch block

choices.label: D
choices.text: Left posterior fascicular block

choices.label: E
choices.text: Nonspecific intraventricular conduction delay

explanation.summary: Right bundle branch block (RBBB) is characterized by QRS >120 ms, rsR' (M-shaped) pattern in V1, and wide, slurred S waves in leads I and V6. The right ventricle depolarizes late via myocardial cell-to-cell conduction rather than through the right bundle.
explanation.whyCorrect: The classic RBBB triad is: QRS >120 ms, rsR' pattern in V1 (reflecting delayed right ventricular activation), and broad S waves in the lateral leads (I, V6) representing late rightward forces.

whyWrongByChoice.A: LBBB shows a broad, notched R wave in I and V6 and a deep QS or rS pattern in V1, which is the opposite of what is described.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Left posterior fascicular block shows right axis deviation with small R in I and small Q in III, without QRS widening or rsR' pattern.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Nonspecific IVCD produces QRS widening without the characteristic bundle branch block morphology patterns described here.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Left anterior fascicular block shows left axis deviation with small Q in I/aVL and small R in III but does not widen QRS beyond 120 ms or produce rsR' in V1.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man with heart failure has an ECG showing a QRS duration of 150 ms, a broad notched R wave in leads I, aVL, and V6, and absence of Q waves in leads I and V6. Which of the following changes in ventricular depolarization best explains the absence of septal Q waves?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Septal depolarization is absent due to fibrosis

choices.label: B
choices.text: Septal depolarization is delayed and masked by left ventricular forces

choices.label: C
choices.text: The septum depolarizes normally but the QRS is inverted

choices.label: D
choices.text: The septum depolarizes from right to left instead of left to right

choices.label: E
choices.text: Atrial repolarization obscures the initial QRS deflection

explanation.summary: In left bundle branch block (LBBB), the left bundle is blocked, so the septum depolarizes from right to left (via the right bundle) instead of the normal left-to-right direction. This reversal eliminates the normal septal Q waves in lateral leads.
explanation.whyCorrect: Normally, the left bundle activates the septum from left to right, producing small Q waves in I, aVL, V5-V6. In LBBB, the septum is activated from right to left via the intact right bundle, reversing the initial vector and eliminating septal Q waves.

whyWrongByChoice.B: In LBBB, septal activation direction is reversed, not merely delayed and masked. The direction of depolarization changes fundamentally.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The QRS is not inverted; it shows a broad notched R wave. The morphology change results from altered depolarization sequence.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Atrial repolarization (Ta wave) can affect the ST segment and PR segment but does not obscure initial QRS deflections.
whyWrongByChoice.A: Septal fibrosis could cause Q waves (from loss of electrical activity), not absence of Q waves. LBBB affects conduction, not septal tissue viability.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman has an ECG showing tall, peaked, symmetrical T waves in leads V2-V4 with a narrow base. Her serum potassium level is 6.8 mEq/L. Which of the following best explains the ECG finding?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Shortened phase 1 repolarization from transient outward current

choices.label: B
choices.text: Prolonged phase 2 plateau from delayed calcium channel closure

choices.label: C
choices.text: Enhanced phase 0 depolarization from sodium channel activation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Increased phase 4 automaticity from diastolic depolarization

choices.label: E
choices.text: Accelerated phase 3 repolarization due to increased potassium conductance

explanation.summary: Hyperkalemia increases extracellular potassium, which increases potassium conductance across cell membranes during phase 3 repolarization. This accelerates repolarization, producing tall, peaked, narrow-based T waves on ECG.
explanation.whyCorrect: Elevated extracellular K+ increases the potassium equilibrium potential and potassium conductance, accelerating phase 3 repolarization. The rapid, uniform repolarization produces tall, peaked, symmetrical T waves with a narrow base.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Prolonged phase 2 plateau would widen the T wave and prolong the QT interval, as seen with hypocalcemia, not hyperkalemia.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Hyperkalemia actually impairs phase 0 depolarization by inactivating sodium channels (raising resting membrane potential), which widens the QRS rather than producing peaked T waves.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Increased phase 4 automaticity affects heart rate and ectopy but does not directly produce peaked T waves.
whyWrongByChoice.A: Phase 1 changes affect the J point and early repolarization but are not the primary mechanism of hyperkalemic peaked T waves.

questions.stem: A 44-year-old man has an ECG showing a prominent U wave following the T wave in leads V2 and V3. His laboratory results show serum potassium of 2.6 mEq/L. Which of the following is the proposed mechanism of U wave formation in hypokalemia?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Delayed repolarization of the Purkinje fibers or mid-myocardial M cells

choices.label: B
choices.text: Early afterdepolarization during phase 2 of the action potential

choices.label: C
choices.text: Rapid depolarization of atrial tissue following ventricular systole

choices.label: D
choices.text: Mechanical stretching of the ventricle during early diastolic filling

choices.label: E
choices.text: Retrograde depolarization of the AV node during ventricular repolarization

explanation.summary: U waves are thought to result from delayed repolarization of Purkinje fibers or mid-myocardial M cells. Hypokalemia exaggerates this phenomenon by prolonging repolarization in these cells, making U waves more prominent.
explanation.whyCorrect: The most accepted theory is that U waves represent delayed repolarization of the Purkinje network or M cells (mid-myocardial cells with longer action potentials). Hypokalemia prolongs repolarization, enhancing U wave prominence.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Early afterdepolarizations occur during phase 2/3 and are associated with QT prolongation and triggered arrhythmias, not normal U wave generation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Atrial depolarization after ventricular systole would represent a P wave, not a U wave.
whyWrongByChoice.D: While mechanical factors may contribute to U waves in some theories, the primary mechanism in hypokalemia relates to delayed electrical repolarization of specific myocardial cells.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Retrograde AV nodal depolarization produces retrograde P waves, not U waves.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old woman has an ECG that shows a QRS complex with a total duration of 90 ms. The QT interval measures 480 ms and the RR interval is 800 ms. Using Bazett's formula, which of the following is the corrected QT interval (QTc)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 420 ms

choices.label: B
choices.text: 537 ms

choices.label: C
choices.text: 480 ms

choices.label: D
choices.text: 460 ms

choices.label: E
choices.text: 600 ms

explanation.summary: Bazett's formula: QTc = QT / √RR (in seconds). RR = 800 ms = 0.8 s. √0.8 = 0.894. QTc = 480/0.894 = 537 ms.
explanation.whyCorrect: QTc = QT / √RR = 480 / √0.8 = 480 / 0.894 = 536.9 ≈ 537 ms. This is a prolonged QTc (normal <440 ms in men, <460 ms in women).

whyWrongByChoice.A: 420 ms would result from an incorrect calculation and underestimates the true QTc.
whyWrongByChoice.C: 480 ms is the uncorrected QT interval. Bazett's correction adjusts for heart rate and yields a higher value at this heart rate.
whyWrongByChoice.E: 600 ms is too high and does not result from the correct calculation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: 460 ms does not result from correct application of Bazett's formula with these values.

questions.stem: A 52-year-old man presents after a syncopal episode. His ECG shows deep, symmetric T-wave inversions in leads V1-V4 with a prolonged QTc of 500 ms but no ST elevation. Troponin is mildly elevated. Which of the following is the most likely pattern being described?

choices.label: A
choices.text: De Winter T waves suggesting acute LAD occlusion

choices.label: B
choices.text: Benign early repolarization variant

choices.label: C
choices.text: Wellens syndrome suggesting critical LAD stenosis

choices.label: D
choices.text: Digitalis effect on repolarization

choices.label: E
choices.text: Right ventricular strain from pulmonary embolism

explanation.summary: Wellens syndrome (Type B/Pattern B) shows deep, symmetric T-wave inversions in V1-V4 and suggests critical stenosis of the proximal LAD. Patients are typically pain-free when the pattern is seen and may have mildly elevated troponins.
explanation.whyCorrect: Deep symmetric T-wave inversions in V1-V4 without ST elevation, in a patient with recent cardiac symptoms and mild troponin elevation, is the classic Wellens syndrome pattern indicating critical proximal LAD stenosis requiring urgent catheterization.

whyWrongByChoice.A: De Winter T waves show upsloping ST depression with tall, symmetric T waves in precordial leads, not T-wave inversions.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Digitalis produces a scooped ST depression ('reverse check mark' or 'Salvador Dali mustache') pattern, not deep symmetric T-wave inversions.
whyWrongByChoice.E: RV strain from PE typically shows T-wave inversions in V1-V3 with right axis deviation, S1Q3T3 pattern, and sinus tachycardia, but the clinical context here points more strongly to Wellens.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Benign early repolarization shows ST elevation with upward concavity and is typically seen in young patients, not deep T-wave inversions.

questions.stem: A 38-year-old man has an ECG that shows a normal P wave, a PR interval of 80 ms, a QRS duration of 80 ms, and no delta wave. Which of the following is the most likely explanation for the short PR interval?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Wolff-Parkinson-White syndrome with a concealed pathway

choices.label: B
choices.text: Atrial premature complex falling in the relative refractory period

choices.label: C
choices.text: Junctional rhythm with retrograde atrial activation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Enhanced AV nodal conduction (Lown-Ganong-Levine pattern)

choices.label: E
choices.text: First-degree AV block with compensatory shortening

explanation.summary: A short PR interval (<120 ms) with a normal QRS duration and no delta wave suggests enhanced AV nodal conduction, historically described as the Lown-Ganong-Levine (LGL) pattern. Unlike WPW, there is no ventricular pre-excitation.
explanation.whyCorrect: The LGL pattern features a short PR interval with normal QRS and no delta wave, implying faster-than-normal conduction through the AV node (possibly via intranodal bypass fibers) without ventricular pre-excitation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: WPW requires a delta wave and widened QRS from ventricular pre-excitation. A concealed pathway conducts only retrogradely and does not shorten the PR interval during sinus rhythm.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Junctional rhythm would show inverted P waves in inferior leads or absent visible P waves, not a normal P wave morphology.
whyWrongByChoice.E: First-degree AV block produces a prolonged PR interval (>200 ms), not a shortened one.
whyWrongByChoice.B: An atrial premature complex would show an abnormal P wave morphology, not a normal P wave with consistently short PR.

questions.stem: A 24-year-old competitive marathon runner presents for a sports physical. His resting heart rate is 42 bpm. He is asymptomatic and has a blood pressure of 118/72 mmHg. ECG shows sinus bradycardia with normal P-wave morphology, a consistent PR interval of 180 ms, and no pauses. Which of the following is the most likely mechanism of his bradycardia?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increased sympathetic tone to the SA node from training adaptation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Structural SA node disease from repetitive cardiac injury

choices.label: C
choices.text: Subclinical myocarditis causing SA node inflammation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Decreased thyroid hormone levels from overtraining syndrome

choices.label: E
choices.text: Enhanced vagal tone leading to decreased SA node automaticity

explanation.summary: Athletic bradycardia results from chronically increased vagal (parasympathetic) tone, which decreases the rate of phase 4 depolarization in the SA node, reducing the heart rate. This is a physiologic adaptation to endurance training.
explanation.whyCorrect: Endurance training increases resting vagal tone, which slows the intrinsic firing rate of the SA node by reducing the slope of phase 4 spontaneous depolarization via acetylcholine's effect on muscarinic receptors and potassium channels.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Increased sympathetic tone would increase heart rate, not decrease it. Training adaptation involves parasympathetic predominance.
whyWrongByChoice.B: SA node disease would present with symptoms (syncope, fatigue) and ECG abnormalities like sinus pauses or junctional escape rhythms, not isolated asymptomatic bradycardia.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Hypothyroidism can cause bradycardia but would present with additional symptoms. There is no indication of thyroid dysfunction in this healthy athlete.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Myocarditis would typically present with symptoms such as chest pain, fatigue, or arrhythmias and elevated inflammatory markers.

questions.stem: A 67-year-old woman with hypothyroidism presents with fatigue and lightheadedness. Her heart rate is 48 bpm, blood pressure is 100/65 mmHg, and temperature is 36.0°C. ECG shows sinus bradycardia. Her TSH is 45 mIU/L and free T4 is 0.3 ng/dL. Which of the following best explains the mechanism by which hypothyroidism causes sinus bradycardia?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Decreased beta-adrenergic receptor expression and sensitivity

choices.label: B
choices.text: Direct toxic effect of TSH on SA node pacemaker cells

choices.label: C
choices.text: Increased vagal efferent activity from hypothalamic stimulation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Autoimmune destruction of the SA node by thyroid antibodies

choices.label: E
choices.text: Reduced cardiac preload from peripheral vasoconstriction

explanation.summary: Thyroid hormones upregulate beta-adrenergic receptor expression and enhance cardiac sensitivity to catecholamines. In hypothyroidism, decreased thyroid hormone leads to downregulation of beta-adrenergic receptors, reducing chronotropic response and causing bradycardia.
explanation.whyCorrect: Thyroid hormone (T3) increases transcription of beta-1 adrenergic receptors in the heart. Hypothyroidism reduces beta-receptor density and responsiveness to catecholamines, decreasing SA node firing rate.

whyWrongByChoice.C: While vagal tone can influence heart rate, the primary mechanism in hypothyroidism is reduced adrenergic sensitivity, not increased vagal output from the hypothalamus.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Autoimmune thyroiditis involves antibodies against thyroid tissue, not against the SA node. Cardiac effects are from lack of thyroid hormone.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Hypothyroidism actually causes decreased cardiac output and diastolic hypertension, but the bradycardia mechanism is primarily from reduced adrenergic responsiveness.
whyWrongByChoice.B: TSH does not have direct toxic effects on SA node cells. The bradycardia is caused by lack of thyroid hormone action on the heart, not by elevated TSH itself.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with anxiety, tremor, and palpitations. Her heart rate is 130 bpm, blood pressure is 150/70 mmHg, and temperature is 38.5°C. ECG shows sinus tachycardia with a rate of 130 bpm. Each P wave has normal morphology and precedes each QRS. Which of the following is the most important distinguishing feature of sinus tachycardia compared to other supraventricular tachycardias?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Heart rate typically exceeds 200 bpm in young adults

choices.label: B
choices.text: Gradual acceleration and deceleration of heart rate

choices.label: C
choices.text: Abrupt onset and termination of the tachycardia

choices.label: D
choices.text: Irregularly irregular rhythm on continuous monitoring

choices.label: E
choices.text: Absence of P waves preceding each QRS complex

explanation.summary: Sinus tachycardia characteristically shows gradual onset (warm-up) and gradual offset (cool-down), unlike paroxysmal SVTs which start and stop abruptly. This reflects the physiologic nature of sinus tachycardia driven by autonomic tone.
explanation.whyCorrect: Sinus tachycardia accelerates and decelerates gradually because it reflects changes in autonomic input to the SA node. This contrasts with reentrant SVTs (AVNRT, AVRT) which begin and end abruptly.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Sinus tachycardia rarely exceeds 220 minus age. In a 30-year-old, the maximum sinus rate is approximately 190 bpm, and rates above 150 should prompt evaluation for other causes.
whyWrongByChoice.D: An irregularly irregular rhythm characterizes atrial fibrillation, not sinus tachycardia, which is regular.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Sinus tachycardia always has upright P waves in lead II preceding each QRS. Absence of P waves would suggest junctional tachycardia or AVNRT.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Abrupt onset and termination characterize paroxysmal supraventricular tachycardia (PSVT), not sinus tachycardia.

questions.stem: A 78-year-old man with a history of sick sinus syndrome presents with alternating episodes of bradycardia (heart rate 35 bpm) and rapid atrial fibrillation (heart rate 150 bpm). He reports syncope during transitions between the two rhythms. Which of the following is the most appropriate definitive treatment?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Oral metoprolol for rate control of the tachycardia

choices.label: B
choices.text: Intravenous atropine for the bradycardia episodes

choices.label: C
choices.text: Permanent pacemaker implantation with AV nodal blocking agent

choices.label: D
choices.text: Catheter ablation of the SA node to eliminate the arrhythmia

choices.label: E
choices.text: Oral flecainide monotherapy to suppress the tachycardia

explanation.summary: Tachy-brady syndrome (a variant of sick sinus syndrome) requires permanent pacemaker implantation to protect against symptomatic bradycardia, combined with an AV nodal blocking agent (beta-blocker or calcium channel blocker) to control the tachycardic episodes.
explanation.whyCorrect: A pacemaker provides a safety net for bradycardia, allowing safe use of rate-controlling medications that would otherwise worsen the bradycardia. This combined approach addresses both components of tachy-brady syndrome.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Beta-blockers alone would worsen the bradycardic episodes and could cause life-threatening pauses without pacemaker backup.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Ablation of the SA node would eliminate all intrinsic SA node activity and is not therapeutic. AV node ablation with pacemaker is a different strategy used for refractory atrial fibrillation.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Flecainide monotherapy could suppress atrial fibrillation but would also suppress SA node function, worsening bradycardia without pacemaker protection.
whyWrongByChoice.B: IV atropine is a temporary measure for acute symptomatic bradycardia. It does not address the underlying SA node dysfunction or tachycardic episodes.

questions.stem: A 56-year-old man on a metoprolol 200 mg daily for hypertension presents with fatigue and exertional dyspnea. His heart rate is 46 bpm and blood pressure is 108/68 mmHg. ECG shows sinus bradycardia. The physician decides to discontinue metoprolol. Which of the following effects on the SA node caused the bradycardia?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Blockade of muscarinic receptors on SA node cells

choices.label: B
choices.text: Inhibition of the If (funny current) in SA node pacemaker cells

choices.label: C
choices.text: Activation of adenosine receptors on SA node cells

choices.label: D
choices.text: Blockade of beta-1 adrenergic receptors reducing cAMP-mediated automaticity

choices.label: E
choices.text: Direct blockade of L-type calcium channels in the SA node

explanation.summary: Metoprolol is a selective beta-1 adrenergic receptor blocker. By blocking beta-1 receptors in the SA node, it reduces cAMP production, decreasing the slope of phase 4 depolarization and slowing the SA node firing rate.
explanation.whyCorrect: Beta-1 receptor blockade by metoprolol reduces adenylyl cyclase activity and cAMP levels in SA node cells. Reduced cAMP decreases If current activation and L-type calcium current, slowing phase 4 depolarization and reducing heart rate.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Muscarinic receptor blockade would increase heart rate (as atropine does), not decrease it. Metoprolol blocks beta receptors, not muscarinic receptors.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Direct If current inhibition is the mechanism of ivabradine, not metoprolol. Metoprolol's effect on If is indirect, through reduced cAMP levels via beta-1 blockade.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Direct L-type calcium channel blockade is the mechanism of non-dihydropyridine calcium channel blockers (verapamil, diltiazem), not beta-blockers.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Adenosine receptor activation slows SA node conduction (as seen with adenosine administration) but is not the mechanism of metoprolol.

questions.stem: A 19-year-old woman presents with fever (39.2°C), cough, and myalgias consistent with influenza. Her heart rate is 120 bpm. Which of the following physiologic mechanisms best explains the relationship between her fever and sinus tachycardia?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Fever suppresses vagal tone through brainstem compression

choices.label: B
choices.text: Fever causes direct SA node injury with compensatory tachycardia

choices.label: C
choices.text: Fever reduces blood viscosity, increasing cardiac output requirements

choices.label: D
choices.text: Fever activates the renin-angiotensin system, increasing heart rate

choices.label: E
choices.text: Fever increases metabolic demand, triggering sympathetic activation

explanation.summary: Fever increases basal metabolic rate (approximately 10-13% per 1°C increase), leading to increased tissue oxygen demand. This triggers sympathetic nervous system activation to increase cardiac output, primarily through increased heart rate. The general rule is approximately 10 bpm increase per 1°C of fever.
explanation.whyCorrect: Fever raises metabolic rate and oxygen consumption, stimulating sympathetic outflow to increase heart rate and cardiac output to meet increased tissue demands. Catecholamine release and direct thermal effects on the SA node both contribute.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Fever does not directly injure the SA node. The tachycardia is a physiologic response to increased metabolic demand, not compensatory for SA node damage.
whyWrongByChoice.C: While fever affects blood viscosity minimally, this is not the primary mechanism. The dominant mechanism is increased metabolic demand requiring increased cardiac output.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The renin-angiotensin system primarily regulates blood pressure and volume, not heart rate. While it may be activated by fever-related dehydration, this is not the primary mechanism.
whyWrongByChoice.A: Fever does not cause brainstem compression. Vagal tone may be reduced reflexively, but this is secondary to sympathetic activation from metabolic demand.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man is found unresponsive after a heroin overdose. His respiratory rate is 6 breaths/min, heart rate is 44 bpm, and pupils are pinpoint. ECG shows sinus bradycardia. After naloxone administration, his heart rate increases to 82 bpm. Which of the following best explains how opioid overdose caused the bradycardia?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Centrally mediated increase in vagal tone and decreased sympathetic outflow

choices.label: B
choices.text: Direct opioid receptor activation on SA node pacemaker cells

choices.label: C
choices.text: Opioid-induced hyperkalemia from rhabdomyolysis

choices.label: D
choices.text: Respiratory alkalosis causing decreased ionized calcium

choices.label: E
choices.text: Opioid-mediated blockade of cardiac sodium channels

explanation.summary: Opioids act centrally on the brainstem to increase vagal (parasympathetic) outflow and decrease sympathetic tone. This centrally mediated autonomic shift slows the SA node firing rate, producing sinus bradycardia.
explanation.whyCorrect: Opioids stimulate mu-receptors in the brainstem, enhancing parasympathetic output via the vagus nerve and suppressing sympathetic outflow. The resulting vagal predominance slows heart rate. Naloxone reverses this by blocking opioid receptors.

whyWrongByChoice.C: While rhabdomyolysis and hyperkalemia can occur with prolonged immobilization in overdose, this is a delayed complication and not the acute mechanism of opioid-induced bradycardia.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Opioid overdose causes respiratory depression leading to respiratory acidosis (not alkalosis), and this is not the primary mechanism of the bradycardia.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Opioids do not block cardiac sodium channels. Sodium channel blockade is characteristic of local anesthetics and class I antiarrhythmics.
whyWrongByChoice.B: SA node cells do not have clinically significant opioid receptors. The bradycardia is mediated centrally through the autonomic nervous system, not by direct cardiac opioid receptor effects.

questions.stem: A 82-year-old woman with a pacemaker set at a lower rate limit of 60 bpm presents with a heart rate of 55 bpm. Interrogation reveals appropriate sensing but failure to capture. Which of the following is the most likely cause of the pacemaker's failure to capture?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Battery depletion causing excessive pacing output

choices.label: B
choices.text: Lead displacement from the endocardial surface

choices.label: C
choices.text: Electromagnetic interference from a household appliance

choices.label: D
choices.text: Oversensing of skeletal muscle myopotentials

choices.label: E
choices.text: Magnet placement over the pacemaker generator

explanation.summary: Failure to capture means the pacemaker delivers a stimulus but fails to depolarize the myocardium. The most common cause is lead displacement (dislodgement) from the endocardium, increasing the distance between the electrode and excitable tissue.
explanation.whyCorrect: Lead displacement increases the distance between the electrode tip and myocardium, raising the stimulation threshold above the pacemaker's output, causing failure to capture despite normal pacing spikes on ECG.

whyWrongByChoice.C: Electromagnetic interference typically causes oversensing (inappropriate inhibition of pacing) or mode switching, not failure to capture.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Oversensing of myopotentials causes inappropriate inhibition (failure to pace), not failure to capture. Here, the device is pacing but not capturing.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Magnet placement typically switches the pacemaker to an asynchronous mode with fixed-rate pacing, which would not cause failure to capture.
whyWrongByChoice.A: Battery depletion reduces pacing output (not increases it) and may cause failure to pace or rate changes, but the question specifies appropriate sensing, suggesting the device is functional.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old man undergoes carotid sinus massage during evaluation of SVT. His heart rate abruptly drops from 160 bpm to 75 bpm with termination of the tachycardia. Which of the following best describes the afferent limb of the reflex activated by carotid sinus massage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Vagus nerve (cranial nerve X) from the carotid sinus to the medulla

choices.label: B
choices.text: Sympathetic chain from the carotid sinus to the spinal cord

choices.label: C
choices.text: Glossopharyngeal nerve (cranial nerve IX) from the carotid sinus to the medulla

choices.label: D
choices.text: Hypoglossal nerve (cranial nerve XII) from the carotid body to the pons

choices.label: E
choices.text: Phrenic nerve from the carotid sinus to the cervical spinal cord

explanation.summary: The carotid sinus baroreceptors send afferent signals via the glossopharyngeal nerve (CN IX, specifically the Hering nerve) to the nucleus tractus solitarius in the medulla. The efferent limb is via the vagus nerve (CN X) to the heart.
explanation.whyCorrect: The afferent limb of the carotid sinus reflex travels via the glossopharyngeal nerve (CN IX) to the medulla. This is distinct from the efferent limb (vagus nerve, CN X), which carries the parasympathetic output to slow heart rate.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The vagus nerve is the efferent limb of the carotid sinus reflex (carrying parasympathetic signals to the heart), not the afferent limb.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The hypoglossal nerve (CN XII) innervates tongue muscles and has no role in baroreceptor reflexes.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The phrenic nerve innervates the diaphragm and has no involvement in the carotid sinus reflex.
whyWrongByChoice.B: The sympathetic chain carries efferent sympathetic signals. Carotid sinus afferents travel via cranial nerves, not the sympathetic chain.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man with symptomatic sinus bradycardia (heart rate 38 bpm) is being evaluated for pacemaker implantation. His ECG shows sinus bradycardia with no escape rhythm. Atropine administration increases his heart rate to only 50 bpm. Which of the following does the attenuated response to atropine suggest about the underlying pathology?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The bradycardia is primarily due to excessive sympathetic withdrawal

choices.label: B
choices.text: Enhanced baroreceptor sensitivity with exaggerated vagal response

choices.label: C
choices.text: The bradycardia is caused by a medication that blocks muscarinic receptors

choices.label: D
choices.text: Intrinsic SA node disease with reduced pacemaker cell function

choices.label: E
choices.text: Concomitant AV nodal disease preventing rate increase

explanation.summary: Atropine blocks vagal (muscarinic) input to the SA node, revealing the intrinsic SA node rate. A poor response to atropine (heart rate remaining below 60 bpm) suggests intrinsic SA node disease (sick sinus syndrome) rather than excess vagal tone.
explanation.whyCorrect: If the bradycardia were purely due to vagal excess, atropine would produce a brisk heart rate increase. The blunted response indicates that the SA node itself has reduced automaticity (intrinsic disease), and the problem is not simply reversible vagal suppression.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Sympathetic withdrawal alone would still allow atropine to unmask a near-normal intrinsic rate. The blunted response points to intrinsic nodal dysfunction.
whyWrongByChoice.C: If a muscarinic-blocking medication were already present, additional atropine would have even less effect, but the key finding is that the intrinsic rate is low even with full vagal blockade.
whyWrongByChoice.E: AV nodal disease affects conduction from atria to ventricles but would not prevent the SA node from increasing its firing rate in response to atropine.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Enhanced baroreceptor sensitivity would cause exaggerated responses to blood pressure changes but would not prevent atropine from unmasking the intrinsic SA node rate.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old man with diabetes and hypertension presents with 2 hours of crushing substernal chest pain radiating to the left arm. ECG shows 4 mm ST elevation in leads V1-V4 with reciprocal ST depression in leads II, III, and aVF. Troponin I is elevated. Which of the following coronary arteries is most likely occluded?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Right coronary artery

choices.label: B
choices.text: Left circumflex artery

choices.label: C
choices.text: Obtuse marginal artery

choices.label: D
choices.text: Posterior descending artery

choices.label: E
choices.text: Left anterior descending artery

explanation.summary: ST elevation in leads V1-V4 corresponds to the anterior wall of the left ventricle, which is supplied by the left anterior descending (LAD) artery. Reciprocal ST depression in inferior leads supports acute anterior STEMI from LAD occlusion.
explanation.whyCorrect: Leads V1-V4 overlie the anterior wall, supplied by the LAD and its septal and diagonal branches. ST elevation in these leads with reciprocal inferior changes is the classic pattern of acute anterior STEMI from LAD occlusion.

whyWrongByChoice.A: RCA occlusion produces ST elevation in inferior leads (II, III, aVF), not anterior leads V1-V4.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Left circumflex occlusion produces ST elevation in lateral leads (I, aVL, V5, V6) or posterior changes, not anterior leads.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Posterior descending artery occlusion produces inferior ST elevation (II, III, aVF) as it supplies the inferior wall.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Obtuse marginal artery supplies the lateral wall, producing ST changes in lateral leads, not V1-V4.

questions.stem: A 71-year-old woman presents with 45 minutes of epigastric pain, nausea, and diaphoresis. ECG shows 3 mm ST elevation in leads II, III, and aVF with ST depression in leads I and aVL. An additional right-sided ECG shows 2 mm ST elevation in V4R. Which of the following complications is this patient at highest risk for developing?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hypotension with nitroglycerin administration

choices.label: B
choices.text: Left ventricular free wall rupture

choices.label: C
choices.text: Acute mitral regurgitation from anterior papillary muscle rupture

choices.label: D
choices.text: Apical thrombus formation from anterior wall akinesis

choices.label: E
choices.text: Development of a ventricular septal defect at the apex

explanation.summary: ST elevation in V4R indicates right ventricular infarction, which commonly accompanies inferior STEMI from proximal RCA occlusion. RV infarction makes the patient preload-dependent; nitroglycerin reduces preload and can cause severe hypotension.
explanation.whyCorrect: Right ventricular infarction renders the right ventricle unable to adequately fill the left ventricle. Nitroglycerin (a venodilator) reduces preload, which critically decreases RV output and can cause profound hypotension or cardiogenic shock.

whyWrongByChoice.C: Mitral regurgitation from papillary muscle rupture occurs with inferior MI affecting the posteromedial papillary muscle, but the question asks about the highest risk related to RV involvement.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Apical thrombus formation occurs with anterior wall MI and apical akinesis, not with inferior/RV infarction.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Ventricular septal defect from infarction can occur with either anterior or inferior MI but is not the most immediate risk uniquely associated with RV infarction.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Free wall rupture is a complication of transmural MI but is not specifically associated with RV infarction or acutely increased by nitroglycerin use.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with exertional chest pain undergoes exercise stress testing. During peak exercise, his ECG shows 2 mm horizontal ST depression in leads V4-V6 that resolves during recovery. Which of the following is the most likely pathophysiologic explanation for the ST depression during exercise?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Transmural myocardial ischemia causing injury currents toward the epicardium

choices.label: B
choices.text: Subendocardial ischemia creating a current of injury away from the electrode

choices.label: C
choices.text: Coronary vasospasm causing transient complete vessel occlusion

choices.label: D
choices.text: Exercise-induced left bundle branch block altering repolarization

choices.label: E
choices.text: Catecholamine surge causing direct ST segment displacement

explanation.summary: Exercise-induced ST depression reflects subendocardial ischemia. The subendocardium is most vulnerable to ischemia during exercise because increased heart rate reduces diastolic filling time and increases oxygen demand. Subendocardial ischemia produces a current of injury directed away from the overlying electrode, resulting in ST depression.
explanation.whyCorrect: Subendocardial ischemia generates a current of injury vector directed from epicardium to endocardium (inward). Surface electrodes overlying the ischemic region record this as ST depression because the vector points away from the positive electrode.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Transmural ischemia produces ST elevation, not depression. ST elevation occurs when the full thickness of the wall is ischemic, creating an injury current toward the epicardial surface.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Coronary vasospasm (Prinzmetal angina) produces ST elevation, not depression, and typically occurs at rest rather than during exercise.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Exercise-induced LBBB can cause ST changes, but the question describes ST depression that resolves with recovery, which is classic for ischemia, not rate-related LBBB.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While catecholamines increase during exercise, ST depression from ischemia has a specific morphology (horizontal or downsloping) that is distinct from catecholamine-related repolarization changes.

questions.stem: A 48-year-old man presents at 3 AM with sudden-onset chest pain at rest. ECG shows 3 mm ST elevation in leads II, III, and aVF that completely resolves after sublingual nitroglycerin. Troponin levels are negative. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Acute inferior STEMI from thrombotic RCA occlusion

choices.label: B
choices.text: Unstable angina with subtotal coronary occlusion

choices.label: C
choices.text: Prinzmetal (variant) angina from coronary vasospasm

choices.label: D
choices.text: Acute pericarditis with inferior lead predominance

choices.label: E
choices.text: Takotsubo cardiomyopathy with basal involvement

explanation.summary: Transient ST elevation at rest that resolves completely with nitroglycerin, occurring in the early morning hours with negative troponins, is characteristic of Prinzmetal (variant) angina caused by coronary artery vasospasm.
explanation.whyCorrect: Prinzmetal angina causes transient transmural ischemia from coronary vasospasm, producing ST elevation that resolves when the spasm relaxes (spontaneously or with nitroglycerin). The early morning presentation, rest onset, and complete resolution are classic features.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Acute STEMI from thrombotic occlusion would not resolve completely with nitroglycerin alone and would produce elevated troponins.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Unstable angina typically shows ST depression or T-wave changes, not ST elevation. Subtotal occlusion does not produce transmural ischemia patterns.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Pericarditis shows diffuse ST elevation with PR depression, not regional elevation confined to inferior leads that resolves with nitroglycerin.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Takotsubo cardiomyopathy typically presents with anterior ST elevation, elevated troponins, and wall motion abnormalities triggered by emotional stress.

questions.stem: A 66-year-old woman presents with chest pain. Her ECG shows ST depression in leads V1-V3 with tall, upright R waves in V1 and upright T waves in V1-V2. Posterior leads V7-V9 show ST elevation. Which of the following best describes the location of the myocardial infarction?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Anteroseptal wall infarction

choices.label: B
choices.text: Isolated right ventricular infarction

choices.label: C
choices.text: High lateral wall infarction

choices.label: D
choices.text: Posterior wall infarction

choices.label: E
choices.text: Apical wall infarction

explanation.summary: ST depression with tall R waves and upright T waves in V1-V3 represent reciprocal changes of a posterior STEMI. Confirmation with posterior leads (V7-V9) showing ST elevation confirms posterior wall infarction, typically from left circumflex or distal RCA occlusion.
explanation.whyCorrect: V1-V3 are positioned opposite the posterior wall. Posterior ST elevation appears as reciprocal ST depression in V1-V3, tall R waves (mirror image of Q waves), and upright T waves (mirror of T inversions). ST elevation in V7-V9 confirms the diagnosis.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Anteroseptal MI shows ST elevation in V1-V3 (not depression) and does not produce tall R waves in V1.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Isolated RV infarction shows ST elevation in right-sided leads (V4R), not the pattern described here.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Apical MI shows ST elevation in V3-V5 with involvement of the cardiac apex, not the reciprocal pattern seen here.
whyWrongByChoice.C: High lateral MI shows ST elevation in leads I and aVL, not the described anterior lead changes.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man had an anterior STEMI treated with percutaneous coronary intervention 3 weeks ago. He now presents with pleuritic chest pain and low-grade fever. ECG shows diffuse ST elevation with an upward concavity. Echocardiogram shows a small pericardial effusion. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Reinfarction from stent thrombosis

choices.label: B
choices.text: Pulmonary embolism with right heart strain

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ventricular pseudoaneurysm formation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Extension of the original infarction

choices.label: E
choices.text: Dressler syndrome (post-MI pericarditis)

explanation.summary: Dressler syndrome is an autoimmune pericarditis occurring 2-10 weeks after MI. It presents with pleuritic chest pain, fever, pericardial effusion, and diffuse ST elevation with upward concavity (pericarditis pattern), distinct from the territorial pattern of acute MI.
explanation.whyCorrect: The timing (3 weeks post-MI), pleuritic chest pain, fever, diffuse concave-upward ST elevation, and pericardial effusion are characteristic of Dressler syndrome, an immune-mediated inflammatory response to myocardial necrosis.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Stent thrombosis would present with acute chest pain and territorial ST elevation matching the original infarct territory, not diffuse ST changes with pericarditis features.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Pseudoaneurysm presents with persistent ST elevation in the infarct territory, not diffuse ST elevation with pleuritic pain and fever.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Infarct extension would show territorial ST changes and rising troponins, not diffuse pericarditis-pattern ST elevation.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Pulmonary embolism produces sinus tachycardia, right heart strain pattern (S1Q3T3, T-wave inversions in V1-V4), and acute dyspnea, not diffuse ST elevation.

questions.stem: A 73-year-old man with an acute inferior STEMI has his ECG reviewed. The emergency physician notes ST elevation in leads II, III, and aVF with ST depression in lead aVL. Which of the following best explains the ST depression in lead aVL?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Reciprocal electrical change reflecting the inferior injury current

choices.label: B
choices.text: Concurrent lateral wall ischemia from left circumflex disease

choices.label: C
choices.text: Artifact from improper left arm electrode placement

choices.label: D
choices.text: Early repolarization abnormality unrelated to the infarction

choices.label: E
choices.text: Left ventricular hypertrophy causing baseline ST abnormalities

explanation.summary: Reciprocal ST depression occurs in leads oriented opposite to the region of injury. Lead aVL (oriented at −30 degrees) is roughly opposite to the inferior leads (oriented around +90 to +120 degrees), so it displays reciprocal ST depression during inferior STEMI.
explanation.whyCorrect: Reciprocal changes are mirror-image reflections of the ST elevation seen in the primary injury zone. aVL, oriented superiorly and leftward, is electrically opposite to the inferior leads and consistently shows reciprocal depression in inferior STEMI.

whyWrongByChoice.C: Electrode misplacement would affect multiple leads and produce inconsistent patterns, not the predictable reciprocal pattern seen here.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Early repolarization produces ST elevation, not depression, and would not selectively affect aVL in the context of inferior STEMI.
whyWrongByChoice.E: LVH strain causes ST depression in lateral leads (V5-V6), but in the context of acute inferior STEMI, isolated aVL depression is best explained as reciprocal change.
whyWrongByChoice.B: While concurrent lateral ischemia is possible, isolated ST depression in aVL during inferior STEMI is most commonly a reciprocal change, not independent lateral ischemia.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old man with known left bundle branch block presents with chest pain. His baseline ECG is available for comparison. Which of the following ECG criteria is most useful for diagnosing acute MI in the presence of LBBB?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Any ST elevation in leads with dominant R waves

choices.label: B
choices.text: Concordant ST elevation ≥1 mm in leads with positive QRS

choices.label: C
choices.text: ST depression in leads V1-V3 of any magnitude

choices.label: D
choices.text: T-wave inversions in leads with negative QRS complexes

choices.label: E
choices.text: QRS duration exceeding 200 ms with notching

explanation.summary: The Sgarbossa criteria help diagnose MI in the presence of LBBB. The most specific criterion is concordant ST elevation ≥1 mm (ST elevation in the same direction as the QRS complex), which is normally discordant in LBBB.
explanation.whyCorrect: In LBBB, ST segments are normally discordant (opposite direction to the QRS). Concordant ST elevation (ST elevation in leads where the QRS is positive) is highly specific for acute MI and is the most reliable Sgarbossa criterion.

whyWrongByChoice.A: ST elevation in leads with dominant R waves is exactly the concordant pattern described in option B, but 'any' amount lacks specificity. The criterion requires ≥1 mm of concordant elevation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: ST depression in V1-V3 is expected in LBBB (discordant changes) and is not specific for MI in this context.
whyWrongByChoice.D: T-wave inversions in leads with negative QRS complexes are concordant T-wave changes, which can be normal variants and are not part of the Sgarbossa criteria.
whyWrongByChoice.E: QRS duration >200 ms may suggest additional conduction disease but is not a criterion for diagnosing acute MI in LBBB.

questions.stem: A 59-year-old woman presents with chest pain. Serial ECGs over 6 hours show evolving changes: initial ST elevation in V1-V4, followed by Q wave development in V1-V3, then T-wave inversions in V1-V4 with ST segment returning to baseline. Which of the following stages of ECG evolution of myocardial infarction is represented by the T-wave inversions with resolved ST elevation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hyperacute phase

choices.label: B
choices.text: Acute phase with full-thickness injury

choices.label: C
choices.text: Subacute (evolving) phase

choices.label: D
choices.text: Chronic (old) phase with completed infarction

choices.label: E
choices.text: Resolution phase indicating successful reperfusion

explanation.summary: The subacute (evolving) phase of MI occurs hours to days after the acute event. It is characterized by resolution of ST elevation, development of pathologic Q waves, and deep T-wave inversions representing ischemic but potentially viable myocardium surrounding the infarct.
explanation.whyCorrect: In the subacute phase, the ST segment returns toward baseline as the acute injury current resolves, Q waves become established (reflecting completed necrosis), and T-wave inversions develop (reflecting ischemia in the peri-infarct zone).

whyWrongByChoice.A: The hyperacute phase shows tall, peaked T waves and early ST elevation before Q wave formation, which precedes the pattern described.
whyWrongByChoice.B: The acute phase shows maximal ST elevation with beginning Q wave formation, not resolved ST segments with T inversions.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The chronic phase shows persistent Q waves with normalized T waves (T-wave inversions often resolve over weeks to months). Persistent T inversions suggest subacute phase.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Reperfusion produces specific patterns such as accelerated T-wave inversions and rapid ST resolution, but Q wave development suggests completed infarction rather than full reperfusion.

questions.stem: A 64-year-old man with a history of anterior MI 6 months ago presents for follow-up. ECG shows persistent ST elevation in leads V1-V4 with deep Q waves. Echocardiogram shows a thin, dyskinetic anterior wall with preserved ejection fraction. Which of the following is the most likely explanation for the persistent ST elevation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Recurrent acute ischemia requiring catheterization

choices.label: B
choices.text: Early repolarization syndrome in the anterior leads

choices.label: C
choices.text: Chronic pericarditis with ongoing inflammation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Left ventricular aneurysm from scar formation

choices.label: E
choices.text: Brugada pattern from sodium channel dysfunction

explanation.summary: Persistent ST elevation months after MI, in the presence of Q waves and a thin, dyskinetic wall on echocardiography, is characteristic of a left ventricular aneurysm. The scar tissue produces a fixed injury current causing persistent ST elevation.
explanation.whyCorrect: LV aneurysm from post-infarction scar produces persistent ST elevation because the dyskinetic, scarred tissue generates a continuous injury current. The thin dyskinetic wall on echo confirms the aneurysm formation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Recurrent acute ischemia would present with symptoms and dynamic ST changes, not persistent unchanged elevation over months with a stable clinical picture.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Chronic pericarditis produces diffuse ST changes and constriction physiology, not focal persistent elevation with Q waves in a coronary territory.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Brugada pattern shows coved ST elevation in V1-V3 with specific morphology and is a primary electrical disease, not associated with Q waves or wall motion abnormalities.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Early repolarization shows concave ST elevation typically in inferolateral leads in young patients, not persistent elevation with Q waves in an infarct territory.

questions.stem: A 42-year-old man presents with acute chest pain. His ECG shows upsloping ST depression at the J point in leads V1-V6 with tall, symmetric, hyperacute-appearing T waves. There is no ST elevation. Which of the following ECG patterns is this most consistent with?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Benign early repolarization

choices.label: B
choices.text: Digitalis effect on repolarization

choices.label: C
choices.text: Posterior STEMI equivalent

choices.label: D
choices.text: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy strain pattern

choices.label: E
choices.text: De Winter T-wave pattern

explanation.summary: The de Winter pattern shows upsloping ST depression at the J point in precordial leads with tall, symmetric T waves, and is a STEMI equivalent indicating acute LAD occlusion. It occurs in approximately 2% of LAD occlusions and does not show classic ST elevation.
explanation.whyCorrect: The de Winter pattern (J-point depression with upsloping ST segments merging into tall, symmetric T waves in V1-V6) is an important STEMI equivalent that requires emergent reperfusion despite the absence of ST elevation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Benign early repolarization shows ST elevation (not depression) with concave upward morphology, typically in young patients.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Posterior STEMI shows ST depression in V1-V3 with tall R waves and upright T waves, which is a different morphology than the de Winter pattern.
whyWrongByChoice.D: HCM strain pattern shows asymmetric ST depression and T-wave inversions in lateral leads, not the described pattern.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Digitalis causes scooped (downsloping) ST depression with a characteristic 'reverse checkmark' morphology, not upsloping depression with tall T waves.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man on digoxin for heart failure has an ECG that shows a characteristic 'scooped' ST segment depression pattern in leads with tall R waves. He is asymptomatic. His digoxin level is 1.2 ng/mL (therapeutic). Which of the following best describes the mechanism of digoxin's effect on the ST segment?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Shortened ventricular action potential duration from increased potassium efflux

choices.label: B
choices.text: Sodium channel blockade prolonging depolarization

choices.label: C
choices.text: Direct myocardial ischemia from coronary vasoconstriction

choices.label: D
choices.text: Calcium channel blockade reducing intracellular calcium

choices.label: E
choices.text: Prolonged phase 2 plateau from enhanced calcium entry
