explanation.summary: Digoxin shortens the ventricular action potential duration by increasing vagal tone and altering potassium handling, producing the characteristic 'scooped' or 'Salvador Dali mustache' ST depression. This is a repolarization effect, not ischemia.
explanation.whyCorrect: Digoxin shortens repolarization by enhancing vagal tone and increasing potassium conductance, which abbreviates the action potential. This produces the characteristic downsloping ST depression ('digoxin effect') that is not indicative of ischemia.

whyWrongByChoice.C: Digoxin does not cause clinically significant coronary vasoconstriction. The ST changes are a direct electrophysiologic effect on repolarization.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Digoxin inhibits Na+/K+ ATPase, which indirectly increases intracellular calcium (not decreases it). It does not block calcium channels.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Digoxin shortens (not prolongs) the action potential duration. Prolongation of phase 2 would lengthen the QT interval.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Sodium channel blockade (class I antiarrhythmics) prolongs the QRS, not the ST segment change seen with digoxin.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old healthy man has an ECG performed for a pre-employment physical. It shows 1-2 mm of ST elevation with upward concavity in leads V2-V5 and a notch at the J point. Heart rate is 62 bpm and there are no symptoms. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Acute anterior STEMI requiring emergent catheterization

choices.label: B
choices.text: Benign early repolarization pattern

choices.label: C
choices.text: Acute myocarditis with precordial ST changes

choices.label: D
choices.text: Brugada syndrome type 1 pattern

choices.label: E
choices.text: Hyperkalemia with early ST changes

explanation.summary: Benign early repolarization (BER) is common in young, healthy individuals and shows concave-upward ST elevation (typically 1-3 mm) with a J-point notch or slur, most prominent in precordial leads. It is a normal variant.
explanation.whyCorrect: In a young asymptomatic patient, concave-upward ST elevation with J-point notching in precordial leads is characteristic of benign early repolarization. The pattern is most common in young men and athletes.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Acute STEMI typically shows convex-upward ST elevation, reciprocal changes, and clinical symptoms. The concave morphology with J-point notch in an asymptomatic young person favors BER.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Myocarditis would present with symptoms (chest pain, dyspnea) and often shows diffuse ST changes with possible PR depression and elevated troponin.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Brugada type 1 shows a coved ST elevation pattern in V1-V3 with specific morphology, different from the concave-upward BER pattern described.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Hyperkalemia initially produces peaked T waves, not J-point notching with concave ST elevation in an asymptomatic patient with a normal heart rate.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old woman with severe aortic stenosis has an ECG showing left ventricular hypertrophy with a 'strain pattern.' Which of the following best describes the ST-T wave morphology of the LVH strain pattern in lateral leads?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Concave-upward ST elevation with upright T waves

choices.label: B
choices.text: Horizontal ST depression with symmetric T-wave inversions

choices.label: C
choices.text: Downsloping ST depression with asymmetric T-wave inversions

choices.label: D
choices.text: ST elevation with tall peaked symmetric T waves

choices.label: E
choices.text: Isoelectric ST segment with biphasic T waves

explanation.summary: The LVH strain pattern shows downsloping ST depression that transitions into asymmetric T-wave inversions (gradual downslope with a rapid return to baseline) in leads facing the hypertrophied ventricle (typically V5-V6, I, aVL for LVH).
explanation.whyCorrect: LVH strain produces downsloping ST depression with asymmetric T-wave inversions (slow descent, rapid ascent) in lateral leads. This is thought to result from subendocardial ischemia due to increased oxygen demand and decreased supply in hypertrophied myocardium.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Concave-upward ST elevation with upright T waves suggests early repolarization or pericarditis, not LVH strain.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Horizontal ST depression with symmetric T-wave inversions is more characteristic of myocardial ischemia than LVH strain, which has asymmetric T inversions.
whyWrongByChoice.D: ST elevation with tall peaked T waves suggests hyperacute MI or hyperkalemia, not LVH strain.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Isoelectric ST with biphasic T waves can be seen in various conditions but is not the classic LVH strain pattern.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with a history of prior MI develops sustained monomorphic ventricular tachycardia during telemetry monitoring. Programmed electrical stimulation in the electrophysiology lab reproducibly initiates and terminates the tachycardia. Which of the following is the most likely mechanism of this arrhythmia?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Enhanced automaticity of ischemic Purkinje fibers

choices.label: B
choices.text: Triggered activity from delayed afterdepolarizations

choices.label: C
choices.text: Early afterdepolarizations from prolonged repolarization

choices.label: D
choices.text: Reentry around a scar-related substrate

choices.label: E
choices.text: Abnormal automaticity from partially depolarized cells

explanation.summary: Sustained monomorphic VT in a patient with prior MI is most commonly caused by reentry around scar tissue. The ability to reproducibly initiate and terminate the tachycardia with programmed stimulation is a hallmark of reentrant arrhythmias.
explanation.whyCorrect: Post-MI scar creates zones of slow conduction and unidirectional block that form the substrate for reentrant circuits. The hallmark of reentry is that it can be reproducibly initiated and terminated by programmed electrical stimulation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Enhanced automaticity cannot be reproducibly initiated or terminated by programmed stimulation. Automatic rhythms show 'warm-up' and 'cool-down' behavior.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Triggered activity from DADs can sometimes be initiated by rapid pacing but is not as reproducibly initiated and terminated as reentry, and is more associated with digoxin toxicity or catecholamine excess.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Abnormal automaticity, like enhanced automaticity, cannot be reproducibly initiated or terminated by programmed stimulation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Early afterdepolarizations are associated with prolonged QT and produce polymorphic VT (torsades), not monomorphic VT.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man on digoxin develops a new arrhythmia. ECG shows atrial tachycardia at a rate of 150 bpm with 2:1 AV block. His digoxin level is 3.2 ng/mL (therapeutic range 0.5-2.0 ng/mL). Which of the following is the mechanism by which digoxin toxicity produces this arrhythmia?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Reentry within the AV node from prolonged refractoriness

choices.label: B
choices.text: Direct sodium channel blockade causing conduction slowing

choices.label: C
choices.text: Early afterdepolarizations from QT prolongation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Parasympathetic stimulation causing atrial automaticity

choices.label: E
choices.text: Delayed afterdepolarizations from intracellular calcium overload

explanation.summary: Digoxin inhibits Na+/K+ ATPase, leading to intracellular sodium accumulation, which via the Na+/Ca2+ exchanger increases intracellular calcium. Calcium overload triggers delayed afterdepolarizations (DADs), which can reach threshold and generate triggered activity causing atrial tachycardia.
explanation.whyCorrect: Digoxin toxicity causes intracellular calcium overload through Na+/K+ ATPase inhibition. Excess calcium activates transient inward currents (via Na+/Ca2+ exchanger and Ca2+-activated channels) that produce DADs. When DADs reach threshold, they trigger ectopic atrial tachycardia.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While digoxin enhances vagal tone and can slow AV conduction, the atrial tachycardia itself is from triggered activity (DADs), not AV nodal reentry.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Early afterdepolarizations are associated with QT-prolonging drugs, not digoxin. Digoxin shortens the action potential duration.
whyWrongByChoice.D: While digoxin does enhance vagal tone, parasympathetic stimulation suppresses atrial automaticity rather than enhancing it. The arrhythmia mechanism is triggered activity from calcium overload.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Digoxin does not block sodium channels. Na+/K+ ATPase inhibition is its primary mechanism, leading to calcium overload and triggered activity.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman presents with sudden-onset palpitations. ECG shows a narrow-complex tachycardia at 180 bpm with no visible P waves. The arrhythmia terminates abruptly with a Valsalva maneuver and the patient feels immediate relief. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: AV nodal reentrant tachycardia (AVNRT)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Sinus tachycardia with high sympathetic tone

choices.label: C
choices.text: Atrial flutter with 2:1 conduction

choices.label: D
choices.text: Multifocal atrial tachycardia

choices.label: E
choices.text: Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response

explanation.summary: AVNRT is the most common paroxysmal SVT. It presents with sudden onset/offset, no visible P waves (retrograde P waves buried in the QRS), narrow QRS, and termination with vagal maneuvers (which slow AV nodal conduction and break the reentrant circuit).
explanation.whyCorrect: The classic features of AVNRT include: abrupt onset and termination, narrow QRS, no visible P waves (simultaneous atrial and ventricular activation), and termination with vagal maneuvers that slow conduction in the slow AV nodal pathway.

whyWrongByChoice.C: Atrial flutter with 2:1 block typically shows flutter waves (sawtooth) at ~300 bpm with ventricular rate of ~150 bpm, and does not typically terminate with vagal maneuvers (it may temporarily increase the block).
whyWrongByChoice.D: MAT shows ≥3 different P-wave morphologies with irregular rhythm, which does not match this regular tachycardia without visible P waves.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Atrial fibrillation is irregularly irregular with no organized P waves. This tachycardia is regular.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Sinus tachycardia has gradual onset/offset, visible sinus P waves, and does not terminate abruptly with vagal maneuvers.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man is being monitored in the ICU after cardiac surgery. He develops a wide-complex tachycardia at a rate of 170 bpm. The rhythm is regular and monomorphic. AV dissociation is noted on careful review. Which of the following findings most strongly supports ventricular tachycardia over SVT with aberrant conduction?

choices.label: A
choices.text: QRS duration of 130 ms

choices.label: B
choices.text: Presence of fusion and capture beats

choices.label: C
choices.text: Heart rate greater than 150 bpm

choices.label: D
choices.text: Right bundle branch block morphology

choices.label: E
choices.text: Regular rhythm with constant RR intervals

explanation.summary: Fusion beats (partial ventricular activation by both the VT focus and conducted supraventricular impulse) and capture beats (normal QRS from a conducted sinus impulse during VT) are pathognomonic for ventricular tachycardia and prove AV dissociation.
explanation.whyCorrect: Fusion and capture beats are diagnostic of VT because they demonstrate that the atria and ventricles are being activated independently (AV dissociation). Fusion beats have intermediate morphology between VT and normal QRS, while capture beats are normal narrow complexes occurring during the wide-complex tachycardia.

whyWrongByChoice.A: A QRS of 130 ms can be seen with both VT and SVT with aberrancy (e.g., bundle branch block). Very wide QRS (>160 ms) is more suggestive of VT but not diagnostic.
whyWrongByChoice.D: RBBB morphology can occur with both VT and SVT with pre-existing or rate-related RBBB. Specific RBBB morphology criteria (Brugada criteria) may help distinguish them.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Both VT and SVT can have regular RR intervals. Regularity does not differentiate between the two.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Heart rate alone cannot distinguish VT from SVT. Both can present at rates above or below 150 bpm.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman presents with recurrent episodes of palpitations. During an episode, her ECG shows a regular narrow-complex tachycardia at 200 bpm with retrograde P waves visible after each QRS complex (long RP tachycardia with RP > PR). Which of the following is the most likely mechanism?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Typical AV nodal reentrant tachycardia (slow-fast AVNRT)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Orthodromic AV reentrant tachycardia via a rapidly conducting bypass tract

choices.label: C
choices.text: Atypical AVNRT (fast-slow) or permanent junctional reciprocating tachycardia

choices.label: D
choices.text: Sinus tachycardia with first-degree AV block

choices.label: E
choices.text: Atrial flutter with 1:1 conduction through the AV node

explanation.summary: A long RP tachycardia (RP interval > PR interval) with retrograde P waves suggests either atypical AVNRT (fast-slow, where antegrade conduction is through the fast pathway and retrograde through the slow pathway) or permanent junctional reciprocating tachycardia (PJRT), which uses a slowly conducting accessory pathway.
explanation.whyCorrect: In atypical (fast-slow) AVNRT, retrograde conduction through the slow pathway takes longer, producing retrograde P waves late after the QRS (long RP). Similarly, PJRT uses a slowly conducting posteroseptal accessory pathway for retrograde conduction. Both produce long RP tachycardia.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Typical (slow-fast) AVNRT has a short RP interval because retrograde conduction through the fast pathway is rapid, producing P waves buried in or immediately after the QRS.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Orthodromic AVRT via a rapidly conducting bypass tract produces a short RP tachycardia because the retrograde conduction through the fast accessory pathway is quick.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Sinus tachycardia would show upright P waves in inferior leads with gradual onset, not retrograde P waves at 200 bpm with paroxysmal onset.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Atrial flutter with 1:1 conduction would show flutter waves at ~300 bpm with ventricular response at ~300 bpm, which is not described and would likely be hemodynamically unstable.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old man collapses during a basketball game. Bystander CPR is initiated and an AED delivers a shock. Paramedics arrive and the monitor shows coarse ventricular fibrillation. Which of the following best describes the electrophysiologic mechanism of ventricular fibrillation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Single stable reentrant circuit producing organized ventricular activation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Triggered activity from early afterdepolarizations in a single site

choices.label: C
choices.text: Enhanced automaticity of a single ventricular ectopic focus

choices.label: D
choices.text: Multiple wandering wavelets of reentry causing chaotic depolarization

choices.label: E
choices.text: Complete cessation of all electrical activity in the ventricles

explanation.summary: Ventricular fibrillation is characterized by multiple wandering reentrant wavelets that continuously fragment and reform, producing chaotic, disorganized ventricular depolarization with no effective cardiac output.
explanation.whyCorrect: VF results from multiple simultaneous reentrant wavelets that propagate chaotically through the ventricles. The wavelets continuously break up and reform, preventing any organized contraction and producing the chaotic, undulating pattern on ECG.

whyWrongByChoice.A: A single stable reentrant circuit produces monomorphic ventricular tachycardia with organized, repetitive QRS complexes, not the chaotic pattern of VF.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Enhanced automaticity from a single focus would produce a regular monomorphic tachycardia, not the chaotic fibrillatory pattern of VF.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Complete cessation of electrical activity describes asystole, not ventricular fibrillation, which has chaotic but present electrical activity.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Triggered activity from EADs can initiate torsades de pointes, which may degenerate into VF, but the sustained VF itself is maintained by multiple reentrant wavelets.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with ischemic cardiomyopathy is found to have frequent premature ventricular complexes (PVCs) on Holter monitoring. The PVCs have an LBBB morphology with an inferior axis. Which of the following is the most likely site of origin for these PVCs?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Left ventricular apex

choices.label: B
choices.text: Left ventricular free wall

choices.label: C
choices.text: Left ventricular posterior fascicle

choices.label: D
choices.text: Right ventricular apex

choices.label: E
choices.text: Right ventricular outflow tract

explanation.summary: PVCs with LBBB morphology originate from the right ventricle (since depolarization travels from right to left, mimicking LBBB). An inferior axis (positive in II, III, aVF) indicates a superior origin. The right ventricular outflow tract (RVOT) is the most common site of idiopathic PVCs with this morphology.
explanation.whyCorrect: LBBB morphology indicates right ventricular origin (the wavefront spreads from right to left). Inferior axis indicates superior cardiac origin. The RVOT is located superiorly and on the right, producing PVCs with LBBB morphology and inferior axis.

whyWrongByChoice.A: LV apical origin would produce RBBB morphology (left to right depolarization) with a superior axis, not LBBB morphology with inferior axis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Left posterior fascicular origin would produce RBBB morphology with left axis deviation, not LBBB with inferior axis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: RV apical origin would produce LBBB morphology but with a superior (northwest) axis since the apex is inferior, not the inferior axis described.
whyWrongByChoice.B: LV free wall origin would produce RBBB morphology since depolarization would travel right to left and then left to right across the septum.

questions.stem: A 38-year-old woman has an ECG showing a narrow-complex tachycardia at 150 bpm that is perfectly regular. Adenosine is administered intravenously. The tachycardia does not terminate, but transiently reveals sawtooth flutter waves at 300 bpm between QRS complexes before resuming. Which of the following is the correct diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Atrial flutter with 2:1 AV conduction

choices.label: B
choices.text: Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response

choices.label: C
choices.text: AV nodal reentrant tachycardia

choices.label: D
choices.text: Sinus tachycardia with anxiety

choices.label: E
choices.text: Junctional ectopic tachycardia

explanation.summary: A regular narrow-complex tachycardia at 150 bpm that does not terminate with adenosine but reveals sawtooth flutter waves at 300 bpm is classic for atrial flutter with 2:1 conduction. Adenosine transiently increases AV block, unmasking the underlying flutter waves.
explanation.whyCorrect: Atrial flutter has an atrial rate of ~300 bpm with 2:1 conduction producing a ventricular rate of ~150 bpm. Adenosine increases AV block temporarily, revealing the sawtooth pattern without terminating the atrial flutter circuit (which does not involve the AV node).

whyWrongByChoice.B: Atrial fibrillation is irregularly irregular, not perfectly regular at exactly 150 bpm.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Sinus tachycardia would slow gradually with adenosine and show normal P waves, not sawtooth flutter waves.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Junctional ectopic tachycardia is rare in adults and would not reveal flutter waves with adenosine.
whyWrongByChoice.C: AVNRT would terminate with adenosine because the AV node is an essential part of the reentrant circuit. The persistence of the arrhythmia after adenosine rules out AVNRT.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old man with COPD and acute respiratory failure in the ICU develops an irregular tachycardia at 130 bpm. ECG shows at least 3 different P-wave morphologies with varying PR and PP intervals. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Atrial fibrillation with coarse fibrillatory waves

choices.label: B
choices.text: Multifocal atrial tachycardia

choices.label: C
choices.text: Sinus tachycardia with frequent atrial premature complexes

choices.label: D
choices.text: Wandering atrial pacemaker with tachycardia

choices.label: E
choices.text: Atrial flutter with variable AV block

explanation.summary: Multifocal atrial tachycardia (MAT) is diagnosed when there are ≥3 different P-wave morphologies, varying PP intervals, varying PR intervals, and a heart rate >100 bpm. It is commonly associated with severe pulmonary disease, particularly during acute exacerbations.
explanation.whyCorrect: MAT is defined by ≥3 distinct P-wave morphologies with irregular PP and PR intervals at a rate >100 bpm. It is strongly associated with COPD, acute illness, hypoxia, and theophylline use.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Atrial fibrillation lacks organized P waves entirely, showing an irregular baseline with fibrillatory waves. MAT has distinct, identifiable P waves of varying morphology.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Sinus tachycardia with APCs would show a predominant sinus P-wave morphology with occasional different P waves, not ≥3 consistent different morphologies.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Wandering atrial pacemaker has ≥3 P-wave morphologies but by definition has a heart rate <100 bpm. When the rate exceeds 100, it is classified as MAT.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Atrial flutter shows regular sawtooth flutter waves at ~300 bpm. Variable AV block causes irregular ventricular response but the atrial activity is uniform, not multimorphic.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old man with WPW syndrome presents with a wide-complex irregular tachycardia at a rate of 250 bpm. The QRS complexes vary in width and morphology. Which of the following medications is absolutely contraindicated in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Intravenous procainamide

choices.label: B
choices.text: Electrical cardioversion

choices.label: C
choices.text: Intravenous adenosine

choices.label: D
choices.text: Intravenous ibutilide

choices.label: E
choices.text: Intravenous amiodarone

explanation.summary: This patient has pre-excited atrial fibrillation (AF conducting over the accessory pathway). AV nodal blocking agents (adenosine, verapamil, diltiazem, digoxin) are contraindicated because they block the AV node without affecting the accessory pathway, potentially causing all AF impulses to conduct exclusively through the bypass tract at lethal rates, degenerating into ventricular fibrillation.
explanation.whyCorrect: Adenosine blocks the AV node but does not slow conduction through the accessory pathway. In pre-excited AF, this removes the AV node as a competing pathway, allowing all atrial impulses to conduct through the bypass tract at extremely rapid rates, potentially causing VF.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Procainamide is a preferred agent for pre-excited AF because it slows conduction through the accessory pathway and may terminate the arrhythmia.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Ibutilide can be considered for pre-excited AF as it does not preferentially block the AV node and may terminate atrial fibrillation.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Amiodarone has multiple mechanisms and while some guidelines caution against its use in pre-excited AF, it is not as absolutely contraindicated as pure AV nodal blockers like adenosine.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Electrical cardioversion is always appropriate for hemodynamically unstable patients and is a safe option for any tachyarrhythmia.

questions.stem: A 75-year-old woman presents with lightheadedness. Her ECG shows a regular rhythm at 40 bpm with P waves marching through at a rate of 80 bpm independent of the QRS complexes. The QRS complexes are narrow (90 ms). Which of the following best describes the location of the escape pacemaker?

choices.label: A
choices.text: SA node with suppressed automaticity

choices.label: B
choices.text: Purkinje fiber network in the left ventricle

choices.label: C
choices.text: Right bundle branch

choices.label: D
choices.text: AV junction (His bundle region)

choices.label: E
choices.text: Ventricular myocardium near the apex

explanation.summary: Complete heart block (third-degree AV block) with independent P waves and QRS complexes is present. The narrow QRS escape rhythm at 40 bpm indicates the escape pacemaker is at the AV junction (His bundle), which produces narrow complexes at 40-60 bpm. A ventricular escape would produce wide QRS complexes.
explanation.whyCorrect: A junctional (His bundle) escape pacemaker generates narrow QRS complexes because it activates the ventricles through the intact His-Purkinje system. The rate of 40 bpm is consistent with junctional escape rate (40-60 bpm).

whyWrongByChoice.A: The SA node is generating P waves at 80 bpm normally. The escape rhythm is from a subsidiary pacemaker below the level of block.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Escape from the right bundle branch would produce a wide QRS with LBBB morphology, not a narrow QRS.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Ventricular myocardial escape produces very wide, bizarre QRS complexes at 20-40 bpm, inconsistent with the narrow complexes described.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Purkinje fiber escape produces a wide QRS complex at 20-40 bpm, not the narrow QRS seen here.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old man develops palpitations while drinking coffee. His ECG shows an early beat with a narrow QRS that is preceded by a P wave with different morphology from the sinus P wave. The coupling interval is 480 ms and the compensatory pause is incomplete (less than two sinus cycles). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Premature ventricular complex

choices.label: B
choices.text: Escape beat from a subsidiary pacemaker

choices.label: C
choices.text: Premature junctional complex

choices.label: D
choices.text: Sinus arrhythmia with rate variation

choices.label: E
choices.text: Premature atrial complex

explanation.summary: A premature atrial complex (PAC) shows an early beat with an abnormal P-wave morphology (from an ectopic atrial focus), narrow QRS (conducted through the normal His-Purkinje system), and typically an incomplete compensatory pause (because the PAC resets the sinus node).
explanation.whyCorrect: The key features are: premature timing, abnormal P-wave morphology (ectopic origin), narrow QRS (normal ventricular conduction), and incomplete compensatory pause (PACs typically reset the SA node, unlike PVCs which usually have a full compensatory pause).

whyWrongByChoice.A: PVCs have wide, bizarre QRS complexes without preceding P waves and typically have a full compensatory pause.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Premature junctional complexes have narrow QRS but lack a preceding P wave (or show retrograde P waves after the QRS), not an abnormal P wave before the QRS.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Sinus arrhythmia shows gradual rate variation with consistent P-wave morphology, not premature beats with different P waves.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Escape beats occur after pauses (when the sinus rate slows), not prematurely. They are late, not early.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with a recent MI has telemetry showing runs of accelerated idioventricular rhythm (AIVR) at a rate of 75 bpm. He is hemodynamically stable and asymptomatic. Which of the following is the most appropriate management?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Observation only, as this is typically benign and self-limited

choices.label: B
choices.text: Intravenous amiodarone loading dose

choices.label: C
choices.text: Emergent cardioversion to restore sinus rhythm

choices.label: D
choices.text: Transvenous overdrive pacing at a higher rate

choices.label: E
choices.text: Intravenous lidocaine bolus and infusion

explanation.summary: Accelerated idioventricular rhythm (AIVR) at 60-100 bpm is a common reperfusion arrhythmia after MI. It is typically benign, self-limiting, and hemodynamically well-tolerated. No treatment is required in stable patients.
explanation.whyCorrect: AIVR is a ventricular rhythm at 60-100 bpm that commonly occurs during reperfusion after MI. It is benign, usually self-limited, and does not require antiarrhythmic therapy or cardioversion. Treatment may be harmful by suppressing a protective escape rhythm.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Amiodarone is not indicated for AIVR. Suppressing this rhythm could unmask severe bradycardia if the sinus node is dysfunctional.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Overdrive pacing is not needed for a benign, self-limited rhythm in a stable patient.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Lidocaine could suppress the AIVR but may leave the patient without an adequate ventricular escape rhythm, risking asystole.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Cardioversion is unnecessary and potentially harmful for a stable, benign rhythm at a normal heart rate. AIVR is not a hemodynamically compromising arrhythmia.

questions.stem: A cardiologist is explaining the three conditions necessary for reentry to occur. Which of the following correctly lists all three requirements for a reentrant circuit?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Enhanced automaticity, triggered activity, and abnormal conduction velocity

choices.label: B
choices.text: Two pathways with different refractory periods, unidirectional block, and slow conduction in one pathway

choices.label: C
choices.text: Prolonged action potential duration, early afterdepolarizations, and shortened refractory periods

choices.label: D
choices.text: Two pathways with identical refractory periods, bidirectional block, and rapid conduction in both pathways

choices.label: E
choices.text: Elevated intracellular calcium, delayed afterdepolarizations, and increased automaticity

explanation.summary: The three requirements for reentry are: (1) two functionally distinct pathways with different electrophysiologic properties (different refractory periods), (2) unidirectional block in one pathway, and (3) slow conduction through the alternate pathway allowing the blocked pathway to recover excitability.
explanation.whyCorrect: Reentry requires: a circuit with two pathways having different refractory periods (creating the substrate), unidirectional block (allowing the impulse to travel in only one direction around the circuit), and slow conduction (allowing time for the previously blocked pathway to recover and be re-excited).

whyWrongByChoice.C: Prolonged action potential duration and EADs relate to triggered activity (torsades de pointes), not reentry.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Identical refractory periods would prevent unidirectional block. Bidirectional block would prevent any conduction. Rapid conduction in both pathways would prevent the circuit from sustaining.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Elevated calcium and DADs describe the mechanism of triggered activity (e.g., digoxin toxicity), not reentry.
whyWrongByChoice.A: Enhanced automaticity and triggered activity are separate arrhythmia mechanisms, not components of reentry.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man presents with progressive fatigue. ECG shows a regular atrial rate of 80 bpm and a regular ventricular rate of 40 bpm. Every other P wave is not followed by a QRS complex, and the PR interval for conducted beats is constant at 200 ms. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: First-degree AV block with sinus bradycardia

choices.label: B
choices.text: Mobitz type I (Wenckebach) second-degree AV block

choices.label: C
choices.text: 2:1 second-degree AV block

choices.label: D
choices.text: Third-degree (complete) AV block

choices.label: E
choices.text: High-grade (advanced) second-degree AV block

explanation.summary: An atrial rate of 80 bpm with ventricular rate of 40 bpm and every other P wave dropped (with constant PR for conducted beats) defines 2:1 second-degree AV block. It cannot be classified as Mobitz I or II because there is no opportunity to observe progressive PR prolongation or constant PR with sudden drop.
explanation.whyCorrect: In 2:1 AV block, exactly every other atrial impulse is conducted. With only one conducted beat between dropped beats, it is impossible to determine if PR would progressively lengthen (Mobitz I) or remain constant (Mobitz II), so it is classified simply as 2:1 block.

whyWrongByChoice.A: First-degree AV block shows prolonged but constant PR with all P waves conducted. Here, every other P wave is dropped.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Mobitz I requires at least two consecutive conducted beats to demonstrate progressive PR prolongation. With 2:1 conduction, this cannot be determined.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Complete heart block shows AV dissociation with independent atrial and ventricular rates. Here, the ventricular rate is exactly half the atrial rate with a consistent relationship.
whyWrongByChoice.E: High-grade AV block describes ≥3:1 conduction ratio (3 or more P waves for each QRS). This is 2:1 conduction.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old woman presents with near-syncope. ECG shows progressive PR interval prolongation from 200 ms to 320 ms over consecutive beats, followed by a dropped QRS complex, then the cycle repeats. The QRS complexes are narrow. Which of the following best describes the typical location of the conduction delay in this block?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Within the SA node

choices.label: B
choices.text: Within the His bundle (intra-Hisian)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Below the His bundle (infra-Hisian)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Within the AV node (supra-Hisian)

choices.label: E
choices.text: Within the bundle branches bilaterally

explanation.summary: Mobitz type I (Wenckebach) AV block with narrow QRS typically occurs within the AV node (supra-Hisian level). The AV node demonstrates decremental conduction, which explains the progressive PR prolongation before the dropped beat.
explanation.whyCorrect: The AV node has inherent decremental conduction properties (conduction slows progressively with each successive impulse until one is blocked). Mobitz I with narrow QRS is almost always at the AV nodal level and is generally benign.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Intra-Hisian block is rare and typically presents with more advanced conduction disease. It does not characteristically show the gradual PR prolongation of classic Wenckebach.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Infra-Hisian block (below the His bundle) is associated with Mobitz type II pattern (constant PR with sudden dropped beats) and wide QRS complexes.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Bilateral bundle branch disease causes wide QRS complexes and typically presents as Mobitz II or complete heart block, not Wenckebach with narrow QRS.
whyWrongByChoice.A: SA nodal block affects the P-P interval (producing dropped P waves), not the PR interval. The P waves here are regular.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man with an acute inferior MI develops Mobitz type I second-degree AV block with a ventricular rate of 52 bpm. He is hemodynamically stable with a blood pressure of 110/70 mmHg. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial management?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Immediate transvenous temporary pacemaker insertion

choices.label: B
choices.text: Intravenous isoproterenol infusion

choices.label: C
choices.text: Intravenous dopamine infusion at high doses

choices.label: D
choices.text: Permanent pacemaker implantation within 24 hours

choices.label: E
choices.text: Close monitoring with observation

explanation.summary: Mobitz I AV block in the setting of inferior MI is typically due to increased vagal tone and AV nodal ischemia (from RCA supply to the AV node). It is usually transient, resolves within days, and does not require pacing if the patient is hemodynamically stable.
explanation.whyCorrect: Inferior MI commonly causes transient Mobitz I block because the AV node is supplied by the RCA (in 85% of patients). The block usually resolves as the ischemia improves. Observation is appropriate when the patient is stable.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Temporary pacing is reserved for hemodynamically unstable patients or those with more advanced block (Mobitz II, complete heart block) that does not respond to atropine.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Isoproterenol increases heart rate but also increases myocardial oxygen demand, which is harmful in the setting of acute MI.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Permanent pacemaker is not indicated for transient conduction abnormalities associated with acute MI. The block is expected to resolve.
whyWrongByChoice.C: High-dose dopamine has significant alpha-adrenergic effects (vasoconstriction) and increases myocardial oxygen demand, which is inappropriate in acute MI.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man presents with syncope. ECG shows a constant PR interval of 160 ms with intermittent sudden dropping of QRS complexes without progressive PR prolongation. The QRS duration of conducted beats is 140 ms with LBBB morphology. Which of the following is the most appropriate management?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Permanent pacemaker implantation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Oral theophylline to enhance AV conduction

choices.label: C
choices.text: Intravenous atropine followed by discharge

choices.label: D
choices.text: Observation with telemetry monitoring only

choices.label: E
choices.text: Carotid sinus massage to assess AV nodal function

explanation.summary: Mobitz type II AV block (constant PR interval with sudden dropped QRS) with wide QRS (LBBB) and syncope indicates infranodal disease with high risk of progressing to complete heart block. Permanent pacemaker implantation is indicated.
explanation.whyCorrect: Mobitz type II block with wide QRS indicates conduction disease below the AV node (His-Purkinje system). This has a high risk of sudden progression to complete heart block and requires permanent pacing, especially when symptomatic (syncope).

whyWrongByChoice.B: Theophylline has limited and unreliable effects on infranodal conduction and is not definitive treatment for Mobitz II block.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Atropine improves AV nodal conduction but is often ineffective for infranodal block (Mobitz II) because the block is below the AV node where vagal innervation is minimal.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Carotid sinus massage could worsen the conduction block and is contraindicated in this clinical scenario.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Observation alone is inappropriate for symptomatic Mobitz II block with wide QRS due to the high risk of progression to complete heart block and asystole.

questions.stem: A 74-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department after a syncopal episode. ECG shows a regular ventricular rate of 35 bpm with wide QRS complexes (160 ms) and a separate regular P-wave rate of 75 bpm. P waves bear no consistent relationship to QRS complexes. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Second-degree AV block, Mobitz type I

choices.label: B
choices.text: Third-degree (complete) AV block

choices.label: C
choices.text: Second-degree AV block, Mobitz type II

choices.label: D
choices.text: Atrial fibrillation with slow ventricular response

choices.label: E
choices.text: Sinus bradycardia with junctional escape

explanation.summary: Complete AV dissociation with independent atrial (75 bpm) and ventricular (35 bpm) rates, where P waves have no consistent relationship to QRS complexes, defines third-degree (complete) heart block. The wide QRS and slow rate suggest an infranodal (ventricular) escape rhythm.
explanation.whyCorrect: Third-degree AV block shows complete dissociation between atrial and ventricular activity. The atria (P waves at 75 bpm) and ventricles (QRS at 35 bpm) are controlled by independent pacemakers with no conduction between them.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Mobitz I shows progressive PR prolongation with eventual dropped beats, maintaining some relationship between P waves and QRS complexes.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Atrial fibrillation shows no organized P waves and an irregularly irregular ventricular response, not regular P waves at 75 bpm.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Sinus bradycardia would show P waves at a slow rate with a consistent PR relationship to QRS complexes. Here the atrial rate is normal.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Mobitz II shows constant PR intervals with intermittent dropped beats. Some P waves are still conducted to the ventricles.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man with an acute anterior MI develops new bifascicular block (RBBB with left anterior hemiblock) and a PR interval of 220 ms. He is currently hemodynamically stable. Which of the following is the most concerning complication that may develop?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response

choices.label: B
choices.text: Development of ventricular tachycardia from bundle branch reentry

choices.label: C
choices.text: Progression to complete heart block from trifascicular disease

choices.label: D
choices.text: Regression to normal conduction within 24 hours

choices.label: E
choices.text: Development of atrial flutter from conduction system irritation

explanation.summary: Bifascicular block (RBBB + left anterior hemiblock) with first-degree AV block in the setting of acute anterior MI suggests disease in three fascicles (trifascicular disease). There is high risk of progression to complete heart block because only one fascicle (left posterior fascicle) remains functional.
explanation.whyCorrect: RBBB + LAHB + prolonged PR suggests conduction delay in all three fascicles. In acute anterior MI, the LAD supplies the His bundle and bundle branches. If the remaining left posterior fascicle fails, complete heart block will occur with a potentially unreliable escape rhythm.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While atrial fibrillation can occur post-MI, it is not the most concerning complication directly related to the conduction system disease described.
whyWrongByChoice.D: While some conduction abnormalities may resolve, the clinical concern with trifascicular disease in anterior MI is progression, not regression. Monitoring for complete heart block is the priority.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Atrial flutter is not directly related to bundle branch disease and is not the most concerning complication in this context.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Bundle branch reentry VT is possible but is a less immediate concern than complete heart block in the acute setting with trifascicular disease.

questions.stem: A 66-year-old woman with complete heart block undergoes permanent pacemaker implantation. Her cardiologist programs a DDD pacemaker. Which of the following best describes the function of a DDD pacing mode?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Paces the ventricle only and senses the ventricle only

choices.label: B
choices.text: Paces both chambers at a fixed rate regardless of intrinsic activity

choices.label: C
choices.text: Paces the atrium only and inhibits when atrial activity is sensed

choices.label: D
choices.text: Paces both chambers, senses both chambers, and can inhibit or trigger pacing

choices.label: E
choices.text: Senses both chambers but only paces the ventricle when needed

explanation.summary: DDD mode: the first D indicates pacing in Dual chambers (atrium and ventricle), the second D indicates sensing in Dual chambers, and the third D indicates Dual response (can both inhibit and trigger). This provides the most physiologic pacing for complete heart block.
explanation.whyCorrect: DDD mode paces both atrium and ventricle, senses activity in both chambers, and responds by both inhibiting (if intrinsic activity is sensed) and triggering (e.g., tracking sensed atrial activity to trigger ventricular pacing after an appropriate AV delay).

whyWrongByChoice.A: This describes VVI mode (ventricular pacing, ventricular sensing, inhibited response), not DDD.
whyWrongByChoice.C: This describes AAI mode (atrial pacing, atrial sensing, inhibited response), used for isolated sinus node disease, not AV block.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Sensing both chambers but only pacing the ventricle would be a VDD mode, which does not provide atrial pacing backup.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Fixed-rate pacing regardless of intrinsic activity describes DOO or VOO mode (asynchronous pacing), which risks competitive pacing.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man with a permanent pacemaker presents with dizziness. ECG shows atrial pacing spikes followed by P waves at 70 bpm, but no ventricular pacing spikes or QRS complexes are seen after most P waves. Native QRS complexes appear intermittently at 30 bpm. Which of the following pacemaker malfunctions is most likely present?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Atrial lead failure to capture

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ventricular lead failure to sense

choices.label: C
choices.text: Battery depletion with mode switch to VVI

choices.label: D
choices.text: Atrial lead oversensing with crosstalk inhibition

choices.label: E
choices.text: Ventricular lead failure to pace (output failure)

explanation.summary: The atrial lead is functioning (pacing spikes producing P waves), but the ventricular lead is not generating pacing spikes. This represents ventricular lead output failure (failure to pace), meaning the pacemaker is not delivering stimuli to the ventricle, resulting in a slow ventricular escape rhythm.
explanation.whyCorrect: The absence of ventricular pacing spikes despite atrial pacing at a rate that should trigger ventricular pacing (in a DDD system) indicates the ventricular channel is not delivering output. This could be due to lead fracture, connector issues, or generator malfunction.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Atrial lead is capturing successfully (atrial spikes are followed by P waves). The problem is with the ventricular channel.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Ventricular undersensing would cause inappropriate ventricular pacing (pacing spikes delivered despite intrinsic QRS), not absence of ventricular pacing.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Crosstalk inhibition would occur when the ventricular channel senses the atrial spike and inhibits ventricular output. However, crosstalk blanking periods are programmed to prevent this.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Battery depletion causing mode switch would change the pacing mode but would still attempt to pace the ventricle, not eliminate ventricular output entirely.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man presents with exertional dyspnea. ECG shows left bundle branch block with a QRS duration of 160 ms, left axis deviation, and a PR interval of 240 ms. His ejection fraction is 30%. Which of the following therapies specifically addresses his conduction abnormality to improve cardiac function?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Cardiac resynchronization therapy (biventricular pacemaker)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Implantable cardioverter-defibrillator (ICD) alone

choices.label: C
choices.text: Radiofrequency catheter ablation of the left bundle

choices.label: D
choices.text: Intravenous dobutamine for inotropic support

choices.label: E
choices.text: Permanent single-chamber ventricular pacemaker (VVI)

explanation.summary: Cardiac resynchronization therapy (CRT) using a biventricular pacemaker is indicated for patients with LBBB, QRS ≥150 ms, EF ≤35%, and NYHA class II-IV heart failure. CRT restores synchronized ventricular contraction and improves cardiac output, symptoms, and survival.
explanation.whyCorrect: CRT addresses the mechanical dyssynchrony caused by LBBB by simultaneously pacing both ventricles, restoring coordinated contraction. Guidelines support CRT for LBBB with QRS ≥150 ms and EF ≤35% with heart failure symptoms.

whyWrongByChoice.C: Ablation of the left bundle would worsen conduction, not improve it. Ablation is used for specific arrhythmias, not conduction defects.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Dobutamine is a short-term inotrope for acute decompensation, not a long-term solution for conduction-related cardiomyopathy.
whyWrongByChoice.E: VVI pacing from the right ventricle would worsen dyssynchrony compared to LBBB and is not appropriate for this indication.
whyWrongByChoice.B: An ICD prevents sudden cardiac death but does not address conduction dyssynchrony. However, CRT is often combined with ICD (CRT-D) in these patients.

questions.stem: A 63-year-old man undergoes electrophysiology study. His HV interval (His bundle to ventricular activation) is measured at 80 ms (normal <55 ms). The AH interval is normal. Which of the following levels of conduction disease does the prolonged HV interval indicate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Disease within the AV node (supra-Hisian)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Disease below the His bundle (infra-Hisian)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Disease within the atrium (intra-atrial)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Disease within the SA node

choices.label: E
choices.text: Disease within the coronary sinus musculature

explanation.summary: The HV interval measures conduction time from the His bundle to the ventricular myocardium. A prolonged HV interval (>55 ms) indicates infra-Hisian conduction disease (disease in the bundle branches or Purkinje system), which carries a higher risk of progression to complete heart block.
explanation.whyCorrect: The HV interval specifically reflects conduction through the His-Purkinje system (below the His bundle recording site). Prolongation indicates infranodal disease, which is the substrate for Mobitz II block and complete heart block.

whyWrongByChoice.A: AV nodal disease (supra-Hisian) is reflected by the AH interval, which is normal in this case.
whyWrongByChoice.D: SA nodal disease affects sinus rate and sinus node recovery time, not the HV interval.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Coronary sinus musculature conduction is not measured by standard intracardiac intervals.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Intra-atrial conduction disease would prolong the PA interval (from P wave onset to His atrial electrogram), not the HV interval.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old man is diagnosed with Lyme disease. He develops progressive lightheadedness and his ECG shows third-degree AV block with a narrow QRS escape rhythm at 45 bpm. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial treatment approach?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Immediate permanent pacemaker implantation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Oral doxycycline alone with close outpatient follow-up

choices.label: C
choices.text: Intravenous antibiotics with temporary pacing if needed

choices.label: D
choices.text: High-dose corticosteroids to reduce inflammation

choices.label: E
choices.text: Radiofrequency ablation of the AV junction

explanation.summary: Lyme carditis causing heart block is typically reversible with IV antibiotics (ceftriaxone). Temporary pacing may be needed for hemodynamic support during treatment. Permanent pacemaker is generally not required because the block resolves within 1-2 weeks of antibiotic therapy.
explanation.whyCorrect: Lyme disease heart block is caused by spirochetal infection and inflammation of the conduction system. IV ceftriaxone treats the underlying infection, and the block typically resolves within days to weeks. Temporary pacing bridges the patient if hemodynamically compromised.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Permanent pacemaker is not needed because Lyme-related AV block is reversible with antibiotic treatment. Implanting a permanent device would be unnecessary.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Corticosteroids are not standard treatment for Lyme carditis. Antibiotics targeting Borrelia burgdorferi are the definitive therapy.
whyWrongByChoice.E: AV junction ablation would destroy the remaining conduction and is completely inappropriate for a reversible condition.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Third-degree AV block in Lyme disease requires IV antibiotics (ceftriaxone) and hospital monitoring with temporary pacing capability. Oral doxycycline alone is for early Lyme without cardiac involvement.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man has an ECG showing alternating RBBB and LBBB on consecutive beats. He has had two syncopal episodes in the past month. Which of the following is the most significant implication of this finding?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Benign conduction variant requiring no further evaluation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Intermittent ventricular pre-excitation from an accessory pathway

choices.label: C
choices.text: Normal rate-dependent bundle branch block pattern

choices.label: D
choices.text: Severe infranodal conduction disease with high risk of complete heart block

choices.label: E
choices.text: Electrolyte abnormality causing intermittent conduction delay

explanation.summary: Alternating bundle branch block (RBBB alternating with LBBB) indicates disease in both bundle branches and suggests severe, bilateral infranodal conduction disease. This carries a very high risk of progression to complete heart block and is an indication for permanent pacemaker implantation.
explanation.whyCorrect: Alternating BBB demonstrates that both bundle branches are severely diseased, with each failing intermittently. This represents the most advanced form of bilateral bundle branch disease and has a very high risk of sudden complete heart block.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Alternating BBB is never benign. It represents the most severe form of infranodal conduction disease and requires urgent pacemaker implantation.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Accessory pathway conduction produces delta waves and pre-excitation, not alternating bundle branch block morphology.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While electrolyte abnormalities can affect conduction, alternating BBB typically reflects structural conduction system disease, not a reversible metabolic cause.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Rate-dependent BBB would show one type of BBB at higher rates and normal conduction at lower rates, not alternating between RBBB and LBBB.

questions.stem: A 76-year-old woman presents with recurrent episodes of near-syncope. Holter monitoring reveals sinus pauses lasting up to 4.5 seconds without escape beats. Between pauses, her sinus rate is 65 bpm with normal P-wave morphology. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Carotid sinus hypersensitivity

choices.label: B
choices.text: Autonomic neuropathy from diabetes mellitus

choices.label: C
choices.text: Vasovagal syncope with cardioinhibitory response

choices.label: D
choices.text: Mobitz type II second-degree AV block

choices.label: E
choices.text: Sick sinus syndrome with sinus arrest

explanation.summary: Sick sinus syndrome (SSS) encompasses a group of SA node disorders including sinus arrest with prolonged pauses (>3 seconds). The failure to generate escape beats during pauses increases the risk of syncope and indicates significant conduction system disease.
explanation.whyCorrect: Recurrent sinus pauses >3 seconds without escape beats in an elderly patient are diagnostic of SSS with sinus arrest. The absence of escape rhythm indicates possible subsidiary pacemaker dysfunction as well.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Carotid sinus hypersensitivity produces pauses in response to neck pressure or head turning, not spontaneous pauses on Holter. No provocative trigger is described here.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Vasovagal syncope typically occurs with specific triggers (prolonged standing, emotional stress) and is not characterized by recurrent spontaneous sinus pauses on Holter.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Mobitz type II shows dropped QRS complexes after P waves with constant PR intervals. Sinus pauses show absence of P waves, not dropped QRS complexes.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Autonomic neuropathy may cause orthostatic hypotension but does not typically produce prolonged sinus pauses without escape beats.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man undergoes sinus node recovery time (SNRT) testing during electrophysiology study. After rapid atrial pacing at 120 bpm for 30 seconds is terminated, the first sinus beat occurs after 2,200 ms. The baseline sinus cycle length is 900 ms. Which of the following is the corrected sinus node recovery time (cSNRT)?

choices.label: C
choices.text: 900 ms

explanation.summary: Corrected SNRT (cSNRT) = SNRT − baseline sinus cycle length = 2,200 ms − 900 ms = 1,300 ms. A cSNRT >525 ms is considered abnormal and suggests SA node dysfunction.
explanation.whyCorrect: The cSNRT is calculated by subtracting the baseline cycle length from the SNRT to account for the patient's intrinsic rate. 2,200 − 900 = 1,300 ms, which is markedly prolonged (normal <525 ms).

whyWrongByChoice.B: 1,100 ms would result from an incorrect calculation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: 2,200 ms is the raw (uncorrected) SNRT. The correction subtracts the baseline cycle length.
whyWrongByChoice.E: 3,100 ms would result from adding the baseline cycle length to the SNRT rather than subtracting it.
whyWrongByChoice.C: 900 ms is the baseline sinus cycle length, not the cSNRT.

questions.stem: A 80-year-old woman with sick sinus syndrome has a permanent pacemaker implanted. Six months later she presents with progressive fatigue and dyspnea. Echocardiography shows new mitral regurgitation and her ejection fraction has declined from 55% to 40%. Her pacemaker is programmed to VVI at 70 bpm and shows 95% ventricular pacing. Which of the following is the most likely cause of her clinical deterioration?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Lead endocarditis causing valvular destruction

choices.label: B
choices.text: Loss of AV synchrony and ventricular dyssynchrony from RV pacing

choices.label: C
choices.text: Pacemaker-mediated tachycardia exhausting the myocardium

choices.label: D
choices.text: Pacemaker battery depletion causing intermittent failure

choices.label: E
choices.text: Progression of underlying coronary artery disease

explanation.summary: Chronic right ventricular apical pacing in VVI mode causes loss of AV synchrony (atrial contribution to ventricular filling is lost) and ventricular dyssynchrony (similar to LBBB). This can lead to 'pacemaker syndrome' with heart failure, mitral regurgitation, and declining ejection fraction.
explanation.whyCorrect: VVI pacing from the RV apex causes two problems: (1) loss of AV synchrony because the atria and ventricles are not coordinated, and (2) dyssynchronous ventricular activation similar to LBBB. Both contribute to reduced cardiac output, functional MR, and heart failure.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Lead endocarditis would present with fever, positive blood cultures, and vegetations on the lead, not isolated heart failure with dyssynchrony.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Battery depletion would cause pacing rate changes or failure to pace, not the described clinical picture of heart failure with high pacing percentage.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While possible, the clinical picture with high RV pacing percentage and new MR is most consistent with pacing-induced cardiomyopathy rather than new coronary disease.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Pacemaker-mediated tachycardia (PMT) occurs in dual-chamber pacemakers, not VVI mode. VVI pacing does not track atrial activity.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man has an ECG showing P waves occurring at regular intervals of 800 ms, but every third P wave fails to appear, resulting in a pause of 1,600 ms. The PP intervals outside the pauses are constant. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Sinus arrest with escape beats

choices.label: B
choices.text: Second-degree AV block, Mobitz type II

choices.label: C
choices.text: Sinoatrial exit block, type II (Mobitz II pattern)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Sinus arrhythmia with irregular PP intervals

choices.label: E
choices.text: Atrial premature complexes with blocked conduction

explanation.summary: SA exit block type II shows sudden failure of an SA node impulse to reach the atrium, producing a pause that is an exact multiple of the baseline PP interval. Here, the pause (1,600 ms) is exactly 2× the baseline PP (800 ms), indicating a single dropped P wave.
explanation.whyCorrect: In type II SA exit block, the SA node fires regularly but intermittently fails to conduct to the atrium. The pause is an exact multiple of the baseline PP interval (2× for a single dropped beat), distinguishing it from sinus arrest (unpredictable pause length).

whyWrongByChoice.A: Sinus arrest produces pauses that are NOT exact multiples of the baseline PP interval because the SA node transiently stops firing rather than firing but failing to conduct.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Sinus arrhythmia shows gradual variation in PP intervals, not sudden pauses that are exact multiples of the baseline cycle.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Blocked APCs would show premature P waves (with different morphology) that are not conducted. The P waves here are regular when present.
whyWrongByChoice.B: AV block affects conduction from atria to ventricles, producing dropped QRS complexes with visible P waves. Here, P waves are dropped, indicating SA-level disease.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man presents with recurrent syncope triggered by shaving over his neck. Carotid sinus massage produces a 5-second ventricular asystole without significant blood pressure drop. Which subtype of carotid sinus hypersensitivity does this represent?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Vasodepressor type

choices.label: B
choices.text: Cerebral type

choices.label: C
choices.text: Mixed type

choices.label: D
choices.text: Cardioinhibitory type

choices.label: E
choices.text: Neurally mediated reflex type

explanation.summary: Carotid sinus hypersensitivity has three subtypes: cardioinhibitory (asystole ≥3 seconds), vasodepressor (systolic BP drop ≥50 mmHg without significant bradycardia), and mixed (both). This patient has asystole without BP drop, consistent with the cardioinhibitory type.
explanation.whyCorrect: A 5-second asystolic pause in response to carotid sinus massage without significant blood pressure drop defines the cardioinhibitory subtype. This is mediated by exaggerated vagal efferent activity to the SA and AV nodes.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Vasodepressor type shows significant blood pressure drop (≥50 mmHg systolic) without marked bradycardia, the opposite of what is described.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Mixed type shows both significant bradycardia/asystole AND blood pressure drop. This patient has asystole without BP drop.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Neurally mediated reflex is a broad term encompassing vasovagal syncope and is not a specific carotid sinus hypersensitivity subtype.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Cerebral type is not a recognized classification of carotid sinus hypersensitivity.

questions.stem: A 64-year-old woman with symptomatic sick sinus syndrome undergoes intrinsic heart rate testing. After administration of both atropine (0.04 mg/kg) and propranolol (0.2 mg/kg), her heart rate is 62 bpm. Her predicted intrinsic heart rate by the formula (118.1 − 0.57 × age) is 81.6 bpm. Which of the following does this result indicate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Normal SA node function with excessive vagal tone

choices.label: B
choices.text: Medication effect on the SA node unrelated to anatomy

choices.label: C
choices.text: Enhanced sympathetic tone suppressing the SA node

choices.label: D
choices.text: AV nodal disease rather than SA node dysfunction

choices.label: E
choices.text: Intrinsic SA node disease requiring further evaluation

explanation.summary: The intrinsic heart rate (IHR) test uses combined autonomic blockade (atropine + propranolol) to eliminate autonomic influences. An IHR below the predicted value indicates intrinsic SA node disease. Her measured IHR (62 bpm) is well below the predicted (81.6 bpm).
explanation.whyCorrect: After full autonomic blockade, the heart rate reflects the intrinsic SA node firing rate. A measured IHR significantly below the age-predicted value confirms intrinsic SA node dysfunction, not merely autonomic imbalance.

whyWrongByChoice.A: If the problem were excessive vagal tone alone, blocking vagal input with atropine would normalize the rate. The persistent low rate after autonomic blockade proves intrinsic disease.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Both sympathetic and parasympathetic inputs are blocked during IHR testing, so sympathetic tone cannot be suppressing the rate.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The IHR test evaluates SA node function specifically. AV nodal disease would affect conduction (PR interval), not the atrial rate.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Atropine and propranolol are the medications used for the test itself. The question implies no other medications are confounding the result.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man with a history of amyloidosis presents with progressive fatigue and a heart rate of 40 bpm. ECG shows sinus bradycardia with intermittent sinus pauses. Which of the following is the mechanism by which amyloidosis causes sinus node dysfunction?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Infiltration and replacement of the SA node by amyloid deposits

choices.label: B
choices.text: Autoimmune destruction of SA node pacemaker cells

choices.label: C
choices.text: Coronary vasospasm reducing blood supply to the SA node

choices.label: D
choices.text: Enhanced vagal tone from autonomic neuropathy at the SA node

choices.label: E
choices.text: Fibrotic scarring from prior myocardial infarction affecting the SA node

explanation.summary: Cardiac amyloidosis causes sinus node dysfunction through direct infiltration of the SA node and surrounding atrial tissue by amyloid protein deposits. This disrupts the architecture and function of pacemaker cells.
explanation.whyCorrect: Amyloid protein deposits infiltrate the conduction system, including the SA node, replacing normal pacemaker cells and disrupting the extracellular matrix. This impairs SA node automaticity and sinoatrial conduction.

whyWrongByChoice.C: Coronary vasospasm is not a feature of amyloidosis. The SA node dysfunction is from direct infiltration, not ischemia.
whyWrongByChoice.D: While amyloidosis can cause autonomic neuropathy, the primary mechanism of SA node dysfunction in cardiac amyloidosis is direct infiltration of the conduction tissue.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Fibrotic scarring from MI is a separate cause of SA node dysfunction. This patient's SA node disease is attributed to amyloid infiltration.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Autoimmune destruction is not the mechanism in amyloidosis. Amyloidosis involves extracellular protein deposition, not immune-mediated cell destruction.

questions.stem: A 75-year-old man with sick sinus syndrome and a dual-chamber pacemaker (DDD) set at a lower rate of 60 bpm presents with fatigue. Pacemaker interrogation shows the device is functioning normally. His atrial rate during exercise only reaches 85 bpm despite maximal exertion. Which of the following pacemaker features should be activated to address his symptoms?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Mode switching algorithm

choices.label: B
choices.text: Rate-responsive pacing (DDDR mode)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Hysteresis programming

choices.label: D
choices.text: Sleep mode with lower rate at night

choices.label: E
choices.text: Managed ventricular pacing algorithm

explanation.summary: This patient has chronotropic incompetence (inability to appropriately increase heart rate with exercise). Rate-responsive pacing (DDDR) uses sensors (accelerometer, minute ventilation) to detect activity and increase the pacing rate to match physiologic demand.
explanation.whyCorrect: Rate-responsive pacing (the R in DDDR) uses built-in sensors to detect physical activity or metabolic demand and increases the pacing rate accordingly. This compensates for the SA node's inability to achieve appropriate exercise heart rates.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Mode switching changes the pacing mode during atrial tachyarrhythmias (e.g., AF) to prevent tracking rapid atrial rates. It does not address chronotropic incompetence.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Hysteresis allows the pacemaker to accept a lower intrinsic rate before initiating pacing, promoting native rhythm. It does not increase exercise heart rate.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Sleep mode reduces the pacing rate at night for comfort but does not address the inability to increase heart rate during exercise.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Managed ventricular pacing promotes intrinsic AV conduction to minimize unnecessary RV pacing but does not address chronotropic incompetence.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with obstructive sleep apnea presents with nocturnal bradycardia. Polysomnography with concurrent ECG monitoring shows sinus bradycardia to 35 bpm and sinus pauses up to 3.5 seconds during apneic episodes, with heart rate returning to 72 bpm during breathing. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial management?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Permanent pacemaker implantation for symptomatic bradycardia

choices.label: B
choices.text: Oral theophylline to increase heart rate during sleep

choices.label: C
choices.text: Initiation of continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP) therapy

choices.label: D
choices.text: Implantable loop recorder for further monitoring

choices.label: E
choices.text: Intravenous atropine infusion during sleep

explanation.summary: Nocturnal bradycardia and sinus pauses in obstructive sleep apnea are caused by hypoxia-triggered vagal surges during apneic episodes. Treatment of the underlying OSA with CPAP resolves the bradycardia in most patients, avoiding unnecessary pacemaker implantation.
explanation.whyCorrect: The bradycardia is secondary to apnea-induced hypoxia and vagal reflexes, not intrinsic SA node disease. CPAP eliminates the apneic episodes, preventing the hypoxia-triggered vagal surges and resolving the bradycardia.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Pacemaker implantation is inappropriate when bradycardia is secondary to a treatable condition. CPAP should be tried first and usually resolves the arrhythmia.
whyWrongByChoice.D: An implantable loop recorder provides long-term monitoring but the diagnosis is already established. Treatment of OSA is the priority.
whyWrongByChoice.E: IV atropine is an acute treatment inappropriate for chronic management and impossible to administer nightly.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Theophylline is not standard treatment for sleep apnea-related bradycardia and has significant side effects including insomnia, which would worsen the condition.

questions.stem: A 78-year-old woman with hypertension treated with verapamil 240 mg daily presents with a heart rate of 42 bpm and lightheadedness. ECG shows sinus bradycardia. She is also taking donepezil for early Alzheimer disease. Which of the following drug interactions most likely contributed to the severe bradycardia?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Both drugs block potassium channels causing QT prolongation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Donepezil inhibits the hepatic metabolism of verapamil

choices.label: C
choices.text: Verapamil enhances the cholinergic effect of donepezil

choices.label: D
choices.text: Both drugs independently decrease SA node automaticity through different mechanisms

choices.label: E
choices.text: Donepezil induces CYP3A4, reducing verapamil levels and causing rebound bradycardia

explanation.summary: Verapamil slows the SA node by blocking L-type calcium channels (reducing phase 4 depolarization). Donepezil, a cholinesterase inhibitor, increases acetylcholine levels, enhancing vagal tone and further slowing the SA node. Both independently reduce heart rate through distinct mechanisms.
explanation.whyCorrect: Verapamil reduces SA node automaticity via calcium channel blockade. Donepezil increases parasympathetic tone by preventing acetylcholine breakdown. The combined negative chronotropic effects from two different mechanisms produce additive bradycardia.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Donepezil is not a significant inhibitor of CYP3A4, which metabolizes verapamil. The interaction is pharmacodynamic (additive bradycardia), not pharmacokinetic.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Verapamil does not enhance cholinergic effects of donepezil. The two drugs act through independent mechanisms at different receptors.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Donepezil does not induce CYP3A4, and reduced verapamil levels would cause tachycardia, not rebound bradycardia.
whyWrongByChoice.A: Neither verapamil nor donepezil are primarily associated with potassium channel blockade or QT prolongation. Their main effect on heart rate is through calcium channels and cholinergic mechanisms.

questions.stem: A 69-year-old man undergoes electrophysiology study for evaluation of syncope. SA conduction time (SACT) is measured and found to be markedly prolonged. Which of the following does prolonged SACT indicate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Prolonged intra-atrial conduction between right and left atria

choices.label: B
choices.text: Delayed conduction through the AV node

choices.label: C
choices.text: Prolonged ventricular repolarization

choices.label: D
choices.text: Delayed conduction through the His-Purkinje system

choices.label: E
choices.text: Delayed conduction from the SA node to the surrounding atrial tissue

explanation.summary: Sinoatrial conduction time (SACT) measures the time for an impulse to travel from the SA node to the perinodal atrial tissue. Prolonged SACT indicates sinoatrial exit delay, a component of sick sinus syndrome.
explanation.whyCorrect: SACT specifically measures the time required for an SA node impulse to exit the node and depolarize surrounding atrial tissue. Prolongation reflects impaired conduction at the SA-atrial junction, which can cause SA exit block.

whyWrongByChoice.B: AV nodal conduction is measured by the AH interval, not SACT.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Ventricular repolarization is assessed by the QT interval on surface ECG, not SACT.
whyWrongByChoice.D: His-Purkinje conduction is measured by the HV interval, not SACT.
whyWrongByChoice.A: Intra-atrial conduction time is measured separately and reflects conduction from the right atrium to the left atrium, not SA node exit conduction.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man with a recent cardiac transplant has a resting heart rate of 95 bpm that does not change with Valsalva maneuver or carotid sinus massage. Which of the following best explains the absence of heart rate response to these vagal maneuvers?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The transplanted heart has denervated vagal and sympathetic innervation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Immunosuppressive medications block muscarinic receptors

choices.label: C
choices.text: The SA node is replaced by a pacemaker lead

choices.label: D
choices.text: Recipient atrial tissue overrides donor SA node function

choices.label: E
choices.text: Coronary artery vasculopathy prevents SA node perfusion

explanation.summary: Cardiac transplant results in complete surgical denervation of the donor heart. Without intact vagal or sympathetic innervation, the transplanted heart does not respond to vagal maneuvers. The resting heart rate is higher than normal due to absence of baseline vagal tone.
explanation.whyCorrect: The transplanted heart is surgically denervated — autonomic nerve fibers are severed during transplantation. Without vagal innervation, maneuvers that normally increase vagal tone (Valsalva, carotid massage) have no effect on heart rate. The higher resting rate reflects loss of tonic vagal inhibition.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Standard immunosuppressive agents (tacrolimus, mycophenolate, prednisone) do not block muscarinic receptors. The lack of response is from surgical denervation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Cardiac transplant recipients typically do not require pacemakers initially. The donor SA node functions, just without autonomic modulation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: In modern bicaval anastomosis technique, recipient atrial tissue does not override donor SA node function.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Early post-transplant, coronary vasculopathy is not yet present. The lack of vagal response is from denervation, not ischemia.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman presents with recurrent paroxysmal SVT. Electrophysiology study reveals typical AVNRT utilizing a slow pathway for antegrade conduction and a fast pathway for retrograde conduction. Which of the following is the definitive treatment?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Long-term oral flecainide therapy

choices.label: B
choices.text: Radiofrequency ablation of the slow pathway

choices.label: C
choices.text: Implantable cardioverter-defibrillator placement

choices.label: D
choices.text: Permanent dual-chamber pacemaker implantation

choices.label: E
choices.text: Long-term oral amiodarone maintenance therapy

explanation.summary: Radiofrequency ablation of the slow AV nodal pathway is the definitive treatment for typical AVNRT, with a success rate >95% and low complication rate. It eliminates the slow pathway that is essential for the reentrant circuit.
explanation.whyCorrect: Typical AVNRT requires both slow and fast pathways in the AV node. Ablation of the slow pathway eliminates the substrate for reentry. This is curative in >95% of cases with <1% risk of AV block.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Flecainide is an option for pharmacologic suppression but is not curative and requires lifelong therapy with potential side effects.
whyWrongByChoice.C: ICD is for life-threatening ventricular arrhythmias, not AVNRT, which is a supraventricular arrhythmia that is not life-threatening.
whyWrongByChoice.D: A pacemaker does not treat or prevent AVNRT. Pacemakers are for bradycardia, not tachycardia management.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Amiodarone is reserved for serious arrhythmias due to its significant toxicity profile. It would be excessive for benign AVNRT.

questions.stem: A 25-year-old man with WPW syndrome develops orthodromic AVRT. ECG shows a regular narrow-complex tachycardia at 190 bpm with retrograde P waves visible in the ST segment. Which of the following correctly describes the circuit of this tachycardia?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Antegrade through the accessory pathway, retrograde through the AV node

choices.label: B
choices.text: Antegrade and retrograde both through the AV node

choices.label: C
choices.text: Antegrade through the AV node, retrograde through the accessory pathway

choices.label: D
choices.text: Antegrade and retrograde both through the accessory pathway

choices.label: E
choices.text: Antegrade through the His bundle, retrograde through the coronary sinus

explanation.summary: In orthodromic AVRT, the impulse travels antegrade (atrium → ventricle) through the AV node-His-Purkinje system (producing a narrow QRS) and retrograde (ventricle → atrium) through the accessory pathway. This creates a macro-reentrant circuit using both structures.
explanation.whyCorrect: Orthodromic AVRT conducts down the normal pathway (AV node → His → ventricles), producing a narrow QRS, then returns to the atria via the accessory pathway. The retrograde P waves visible in the ST segment represent atrial activation via the bypass tract.

whyWrongByChoice.A: This describes antidromic AVRT, which conducts antegrade through the accessory pathway (producing a wide, pre-excited QRS), not a narrow-complex tachycardia.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Conduction through the accessory pathway in both directions is not a described tachycardia circuit.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The coronary sinus is an anatomic venous structure, not a conduction pathway. Some accessory pathways may be located near the coronary sinus but conduction is through the pathway itself.
