whyWrongByChoice.B: Both antegrade and retrograde through the AV node describes AVNRT (using slow and fast pathways within the AV node), not AVRT which uses an accessory pathway.

questions.stem: A 52-year-old man presents with palpitations and a regular tachycardia at 150 bpm. After IV adenosine, the ventricular rate transiently slows to reveal sawtooth flutter waves at 300 bpm in leads II, III, and aVF, best described as negative (inverted) in these leads. Which of the following best describes the reentrant circuit of this arrhythmia?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Macro-reentry involving both atria via Bachmann bundle

choices.label: B
choices.text: Clockwise reentry in the left atrium around the mitral annulus

choices.label: C
choices.text: Micro-reentry within the AV node using dual pathways

choices.label: D
choices.text: Counterclockwise reentry in the right atrium around the tricuspid annulus

choices.label: E
choices.text: Focal automatic tachycardia from the right atrial appendage

explanation.summary: Typical atrial flutter involves a counterclockwise (CCW) macro-reentrant circuit in the right atrium around the tricuspid annulus, passing through the cavotricuspid isthmus (CTI). Inverted (negative) flutter waves in inferior leads are characteristic of this counterclockwise rotation.
explanation.whyCorrect: Typical (CTI-dependent) atrial flutter with counterclockwise rotation produces negative (inverted) sawtooth flutter waves in inferior leads (II, III, aVF) because the atrial activation wavefront travels superiorly along the interatrial septum (toward the positive electrode of the inferior leads), then inferiorly along the right atrial free wall.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Left atrial flutter around the mitral annulus is an atypical flutter circuit and does not produce the classic negative sawtooth waves in inferior leads.
whyWrongByChoice.C: AV nodal reentry (AVNRT) is a different arrhythmia with a much smaller reentrant circuit that does not produce flutter waves.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Focal atrial tachycardia is an automatic or triggered rhythm, not a reentrant circuit, and does not produce sawtooth flutter waves.
whyWrongByChoice.A: A macro-reentrant circuit involving both atria is not the typical flutter mechanism. Typical flutter is confined to the right atrium.

questions.stem: A 48-year-old woman with recurrent typical atrial flutter refractory to rate control medications is referred for catheter ablation. Which of the following anatomic structures is the target for ablation to cure typical atrial flutter?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Pulmonary vein ostia

choices.label: B
choices.text: Crista terminalis

choices.label: C
choices.text: AV nodal slow pathway

choices.label: D
choices.text: Bundle of Kent (accessory pathway)

choices.label: E
choices.text: Cavotricuspid isthmus

explanation.summary: The cavotricuspid isthmus (CTI) is the critical narrow corridor of the typical atrial flutter circuit, located between the tricuspid annulus and the inferior vena cava. Ablation across the CTI creates a line of conduction block that interrupts the circuit with >95% success rate.
explanation.whyCorrect: The CTI is the obligatory conduction pathway for the typical flutter circuit. Creating a complete line of bidirectional block across the CTI eliminates the circuit and cures typical atrial flutter.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Pulmonary vein isolation is the ablation target for atrial fibrillation, not atrial flutter.
whyWrongByChoice.C: AV nodal slow pathway ablation treats AVNRT, not atrial flutter.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Bundle of Kent ablation treats WPW syndrome and AVRT, not atrial flutter.
whyWrongByChoice.B: The crista terminalis serves as a boundary for the flutter circuit but is not the target for ablation. The CTI is the critical isthmus.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman with WPW syndrome develops a wide-complex irregular tachycardia at 220 bpm. She is hemodynamically unstable with a blood pressure of 80/50 mmHg. Which of the following is the most appropriate immediate treatment?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Synchronized cardioversion starting at 100-200 J biphasic

choices.label: B
choices.text: Intravenous verapamil 5 mg over 2 minutes

choices.label: C
choices.text: Intravenous adenosine 12 mg rapid push

choices.label: D
choices.text: Intravenous digoxin 0.5 mg loading dose

choices.label: E
choices.text: Intravenous diltiazem 20 mg bolus

explanation.summary: A hemodynamically unstable patient with pre-excited atrial fibrillation requires immediate synchronized electrical cardioversion. AV nodal blocking agents are contraindicated as they may accelerate conduction through the accessory pathway and cause ventricular fibrillation.
explanation.whyCorrect: Hemodynamic instability mandates immediate cardioversion regardless of the rhythm. Additionally, all AV nodal blockers (adenosine, verapamil, diltiazem, digoxin) are contraindicated in pre-excited AF because they may facilitate 1:1 conduction through the bypass tract.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Verapamil blocks the AV node and may paradoxically increase the ventricular rate by preferentially conducting through the bypass tract.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Digoxin enhances vagal tone at the AV node and may shorten the refractory period of the accessory pathway, increasing the risk of VF.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Diltiazem, like verapamil, blocks the AV node and is contraindicated in pre-excited atrial fibrillation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Adenosine blocks the AV node, potentially redirecting all conduction through the accessory pathway and causing ventricular fibrillation.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old woman develops a narrow-complex tachycardia after cardiac surgery. ECG shows a regular atrial rate of 180 bpm with a warm-up pattern (gradual rate acceleration at onset) and a cool-down pattern (gradual deceleration at termination). The arrhythmia does not terminate with adenosine but transiently shows AV block. Which of the following is the most likely mechanism?

choices.label: A
choices.text: AV nodal reentrant tachycardia

choices.label: B
choices.text: Focal atrial tachycardia from enhanced automaticity

choices.label: C
choices.text: Orthodromic AV reentrant tachycardia

choices.label: D
choices.text: Typical atrial flutter with 1:1 conduction

choices.label: E
choices.text: Sinus tachycardia from postoperative pain

explanation.summary: Focal atrial tachycardia from enhanced automaticity characteristically shows warm-up and cool-down behavior (gradual onset and offset). It does not terminate with adenosine or vagal maneuvers but AV block may occur (proving the AV node is not part of the circuit). Post-surgical inflammation is a common trigger.
explanation.whyCorrect: Automatic atrial tachycardia originates from a single focus with enhanced automaticity. The warm-up/cool-down pattern is pathognomonic for automatic rhythms. AV block without termination proves the tachycardia originates above the AV node independently.

whyWrongByChoice.A: AVNRT terminates with adenosine because the AV node is part of the reentrant circuit. This tachycardia persisted through AV block, excluding AVNRT.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Orthodromic AVRT also terminates with adenosine (AV node is essential to the circuit). The persistence through AV block excludes AVRT.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Atrial flutter is a macro-reentrant arrhythmia with abrupt onset and fixed atrial rate (typically 300 bpm). It does not show warm-up/cool-down behavior.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Sinus tachycardia shows normal P-wave morphology and would respond to vagal maneuvers with gradual slowing. An atrial rate of 180 with distinct onset behavior suggests a focal atrial tachycardia.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old man with WPW syndrome has his accessory pathway localized by surface ECG. The delta wave is negative in leads II, III, and aVF, and positive in leads I and aVL. Which of the following is the most likely location of the accessory pathway?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Left lateral free wall

choices.label: B
choices.text: Right posteroseptal region

choices.label: C
choices.text: Left posteroseptal region

choices.label: D
choices.text: Right anteroseptal region

choices.label: E
choices.text: Right lateral free wall

explanation.summary: A negative delta wave in inferior leads (II, III, aVF) indicates that pre-excitation begins at the inferior/posterior aspect of the heart, directing the initial activation superiorly. A positive delta wave in lead I and aVL suggests the pathway is on the left side (septal). Together, these findings localize the pathway to the left posteroseptal region.
explanation.whyCorrect: Negative delta waves in inferior leads indicate inferior/posterior pathway location (activation moves away from inferior leads). Positive delta in I and aVL indicates left-sided activation. The left posteroseptal region fits both criteria.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Left lateral pathways produce positive delta waves in inferior leads (activation directed inferiorly) and may show negative delta in aVL.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Right posteroseptal pathways would show negative inferior delta waves but typically negative or isoelectric delta in lead I (rightward activation), not positive.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Right anteroseptal pathways produce negative delta in V1-V2 and typically positive inferior delta waves.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Right lateral free wall pathways would produce LBBB-like pre-excitation pattern with positive inferior delta waves.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man presents with palpitations. ECG shows a regular narrow-complex tachycardia at 160 bpm. After adenosine administration, the tachycardia terminates with the last visible complex being a P wave (block in the retrograde limb). Which of the following tachycardia mechanisms is most consistent with termination in this manner?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Focal atrial tachycardia from the left atrium

choices.label: B
choices.text: Sinus tachycardia from catecholamine excess

choices.label: C
choices.text: Atrial flutter with variable block

choices.label: D
choices.text: AV nodal reentrant tachycardia (AVNRT)

choices.label: E
choices.text: Inappropriate sinus tachycardia

explanation.summary: When a tachycardia terminates with a P wave (retrograde block) after adenosine, it indicates the AV node is part of the tachycardia circuit. AVNRT terminates when adenosine blocks conduction in either the slow or fast AV nodal pathway, breaking the circuit. Termination after a retrograde P wave confirms the last event was atrial activation before the circuit was interrupted.
explanation.whyCorrect: AVNRT uses the AV node as both the antegrade and retrograde limb. Adenosine blocks AV nodal conduction, terminating the circuit. Termination with a P wave (block occurred in the antegrade limb after the last retrograde P was generated) is characteristic of AV node-dependent tachycardias.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Focal atrial tachycardia does not require the AV node for perpetuation, so adenosine causes AV block without terminating the tachycardia.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Sinus tachycardia may transiently slow with adenosine but does not terminate abruptly with a P wave.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Inappropriate sinus tachycardia is a persistent sinus rhythm abnormality that does not terminate with a single adenosine dose.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Atrial flutter does not terminate with adenosine because the reentrant circuit is in the atrium, not the AV node. Adenosine only increases AV block temporarily.

questions.stem: A 42-year-old woman has recurrent episodes of SVT that are not controlled with metoprolol. She wishes to avoid catheter ablation. Her physician prescribes flecainide as a 'pill-in-the-pocket' approach. Which of the following conditions must be excluded before prescribing flecainide for this indication?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Asthma or reactive airway disease

choices.label: B
choices.text: Chronic kidney disease stage 2 or higher

choices.label: C
choices.text: Hepatic insufficiency with elevated bilirubin

choices.label: D
choices.text: History of migraine headaches with aura

choices.label: E
choices.text: Structural heart disease or coronary artery disease

explanation.summary: Flecainide (a class IC antiarrhythmic) is contraindicated in patients with structural heart disease or coronary artery disease. The CAST trial demonstrated increased mortality with class IC agents in post-MI patients. Flecainide can only be used safely in patients with structurally normal hearts.
explanation.whyCorrect: The CAST trial showed that flecainide and encainide increased mortality in patients with prior MI. Structural heart disease (reduced EF, LVH, coronary disease) increases the risk of proarrhythmia with class IC agents, making them contraindicated in these patients.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Asthma is a concern with beta-blockers, not flecainide. Flecainide has no significant bronchospastic effects.
whyWrongByChoice.C: While flecainide is hepatically metabolized, mild-moderate hepatic disease is not an absolute contraindication. The critical exclusion is structural heart disease.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Migraine headaches have no interaction with flecainide therapy.
whyWrongByChoice.B: While dose adjustment may be needed in severe renal impairment, CKD stage 2 is not a contraindication to flecainide.

questions.stem: A 33-year-old man presents with a wide-complex tachycardia at 210 bpm. ECG shows a regular tachycardia with QRS duration of 180 ms and a pre-excited (delta wave) morphology. His WPW syndrome is known. Which of the following best describes this arrhythmia?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Antidromic AVRT conducting antegrade through the accessory pathway

choices.label: B
choices.text: Orthodromic AVRT with rate-related bundle branch block

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ventricular tachycardia arising from the right ventricle

choices.label: D
choices.text: AVNRT with bystander accessory pathway

choices.label: E
choices.text: Atrial fibrillation with 1:1 accessory pathway conduction

explanation.summary: Antidromic AVRT conducts antegrade through the accessory pathway (producing maximally pre-excited, wide QRS complexes) and retrograde through the AV node. It presents as a regular wide-complex tachycardia with delta wave morphology.
explanation.whyCorrect: In antidromic AVRT, the impulse travels from atrium to ventricle via the accessory pathway (antegrade, producing the wide pre-excited QRS) and returns from ventricle to atrium via the AV node (retrograde). The rhythm is regular, distinguishing it from pre-excited AF.

whyWrongByChoice.C: VT produces wide QRS without delta waves. The known WPW and delta wave morphology favor antidromic AVRT.
whyWrongByChoice.D: A bystander accessory pathway during AVNRT could produce pre-excitation, but AVNRT with maximal pre-excitation at 210 bpm is much less likely than antidromic AVRT in known WPW.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Pre-excited AF is irregular, not regular. The regular rhythm excludes atrial fibrillation.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Orthodromic AVRT conducts antegrade through the AV node, producing narrow QRS complexes. Rate-related BBB would widen the QRS but would not produce delta waves.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman presents with palpitations. Her ECG shows a narrow-complex tachycardia at 175 bpm. Pseudo r' waves are noted in lead V1 and pseudo S waves are seen in leads II, III, and aVF that were not present on her baseline ECG. Which of the following arrhythmias do these ECG features most strongly suggest?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Sinus tachycardia with rate-related changes

choices.label: B
choices.text: Typical (slow-fast) AV nodal reentrant tachycardia

choices.label: C
choices.text: Orthodromic AV reentrant tachycardia via a left lateral pathway

choices.label: D
choices.text: Focal atrial tachycardia from the right atrium

choices.label: E
choices.text: Atrial flutter with 2:1 conduction

explanation.summary: Pseudo r' waves in V1 and pseudo S waves in inferior leads represent retrograde P waves buried at the terminal portion of the QRS. These are characteristic of typical (slow-fast) AVNRT, where near-simultaneous atrial and ventricular activation causes the retrograde P wave to subtly distort the QRS complex.
explanation.whyCorrect: In typical AVNRT, retrograde atrial activation via the fast pathway occurs nearly simultaneously with ventricular activation, creating a pseudo r' in V1 (retrograde P wave mimicking a small r' wave) and pseudo S waves in inferior leads (retrograde P waves creating terminal negative deflections).

whyWrongByChoice.A: Sinus tachycardia has upright P waves preceding the QRS, not pseudo r' or pseudo S waves distorting the terminal QRS.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Orthodromic AVRT via a left lateral pathway shows retrograde P waves well after the QRS (in the ST segment) because retrograde conduction through the distant accessory pathway takes longer.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Focal atrial tachycardia shows distinct P waves (often with different morphology from sinus) before each QRS, not pseudo deflections within the QRS.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Atrial flutter shows sawtooth flutter waves at ~300 bpm, not pseudo r' or pseudo S waves.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old man with WPW syndrome asks about the risk of sudden cardiac death. Electrophysiology study shows that the shortest pre-excited RR interval during induced atrial fibrillation is 210 ms. Which of the following does this finding indicate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The accessory pathway has a long refractory period and low risk of SCD

choices.label: B
choices.text: The AV node has enhanced conduction capacity

choices.label: C
choices.text: The accessory pathway conducts rapidly and poses a high risk of SCD

choices.label: D
choices.text: The patient will develop AVNRT rather than AVRT

choices.label: E
choices.text: The accessory pathway is incapable of antegrade conduction

explanation.summary: The shortest pre-excited RR interval (SPERRI) during AF reflects the antegrade refractory period of the accessory pathway. A SPERRI ≤250 ms indicates a short refractory period with rapid conduction capability, placing the patient at high risk for ventricular fibrillation and sudden cardiac death.
explanation.whyCorrect: SPERRI of 210 ms (≤250 ms) indicates the accessory pathway can conduct very rapidly during AF, potentially producing ventricular rates >285 bpm (60,000/210). Such rapid rates can degenerate into ventricular fibrillation, making this a high-risk pathway requiring ablation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: A SPERRI of 210 ms indicates a short (not long) refractory period. Longer refractory periods (SPERRI >250 ms) indicate lower risk.
whyWrongByChoice.D: SPERRI does not predict which type of tachycardia will develop. It assesses the risk during atrial fibrillation specifically.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A measurable SPERRI during AF demonstrates that the pathway conducts antegradely. A concealed pathway (retrograde-only) would not produce pre-excited RR intervals.
whyWrongByChoice.B: SPERRI measures accessory pathway conduction, not AV nodal conduction.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old woman is diagnosed with inappropriate sinus tachycardia (IST) after extensive workup excludes secondary causes. Her resting heart rate is consistently 110-120 bpm with normal P-wave morphology. Beta-blockers provide incomplete relief. Which of the following medications specifically targets the mechanism of IST?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Digoxin to enhance vagal tone

choices.label: B
choices.text: Flecainide to block sodium channels

choices.label: C
choices.text: Amiodarone to prolong the action potential duration

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ivabradine to inhibit the If (funny) current

choices.label: E
choices.text: Adenosine to transiently block the AV node

explanation.summary: Ivabradine selectively inhibits the If (funny) current in the SA node, which is responsible for phase 4 spontaneous depolarization. By slowing the diastolic depolarization rate, it directly reduces heart rate without affecting blood pressure or contractility, making it ideal for IST.
explanation.whyCorrect: The If current drives spontaneous diastolic depolarization in SA node cells. Ivabradine is a selective If inhibitor that slows heart rate without other cardiovascular effects. It specifically targets the mechanism of IST (enhanced SA node automaticity).

whyWrongByChoice.A: Digoxin enhances vagal tone and is used primarily for rate control in AF and heart failure. It is not first-line for IST and has a narrow therapeutic window.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Amiodarone is a broad-spectrum antiarrhythmic with significant toxicity. It is excessive for IST and not specifically targeted to the mechanism.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Adenosine has ultra-short action and is used for acute SVT termination, not chronic IST management.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Flecainide blocks sodium channels and is used for SVT prevention. It does not specifically target the If current mechanism of IST.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with a history of prior mitral valve surgery develops a new atrial tachycardia. Electrophysiology study reveals a macro-reentrant circuit in the left atrium related to the surgical scar. Which of the following best classifies this arrhythmia?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Typical cavotricuspid isthmus-dependent atrial flutter

choices.label: B
choices.text: AV nodal reentrant tachycardia

choices.label: C
choices.text: Focal atrial tachycardia from triggered activity

choices.label: D
choices.text: Multifocal atrial tachycardia

choices.label: E
choices.text: Atypical (non-CTI-dependent) atrial flutter

explanation.summary: Macro-reentrant atrial tachycardia involving the left atrium around a surgical scar is classified as atypical (non-CTI-dependent) atrial flutter. Unlike typical flutter, which uses the cavotricuspid isthmus in the right atrium, atypical flutter circuits occur around anatomic obstacles (scars, valves) in either atrium.
explanation.whyCorrect: Post-surgical left atrial macro-reentrant tachycardia is classified as atypical flutter because it does not involve the cavotricuspid isthmus. The surgical scar creates slow conduction zones that support the reentrant circuit.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Typical flutter is CTI-dependent and occurs in the right atrium. This is a left atrial scar-related circuit.
whyWrongByChoice.C: This is a macro-reentrant circuit, not a focal automatic or triggered mechanism. Focal tachycardias originate from a single point.
whyWrongByChoice.D: MAT shows ≥3 P-wave morphologies from multiple foci. This is a single organized reentrant circuit.
whyWrongByChoice.B: AVNRT is a micro-reentrant circuit within the AV node, not a left atrial macro-reentrant circuit.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with ischemic cardiomyopathy and an ejection fraction of 25% receives an ICD for primary prevention. One month later, he receives an appropriate ICD shock. Device interrogation shows sustained monomorphic ventricular tachycardia at 190 bpm terminated by the shock. Which of the following is the most common mechanism of monomorphic VT in ischemic cardiomyopathy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Scar-related reentry through surviving myocardial bundles

choices.label: B
choices.text: Enhanced automaticity in surviving Purkinje fibers

choices.label: C
choices.text: Triggered activity from delayed afterdepolarizations

choices.label: D
choices.text: Early afterdepolarizations from prolonged repolarization

choices.label: E
choices.text: Abnormal automaticity from partially depolarized myocytes

explanation.summary: In ischemic cardiomyopathy, scar tissue from prior MI creates areas of slow conduction through surviving myocardial bundles interspersed with fibrosis. These bundles form the substrate for reentrant circuits producing sustained monomorphic VT.
explanation.whyCorrect: Post-MI scar contains surviving myocardial fibers that conduct slowly and create zones of unidirectional block — the perfect substrate for reentrant VT. The fixed scar anatomy produces the same reentrant circuit each time, resulting in monomorphic QRS morphology.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Enhanced Purkinje fiber automaticity can cause accelerated idioventricular rhythm but is not the primary mechanism of sustained monomorphic VT in chronic ischemic cardiomyopathy.
whyWrongByChoice.D: EADs cause polymorphic VT (torsades de pointes) in the setting of QT prolongation, not monomorphic VT from scar.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Abnormal automaticity may contribute to arrhythmias in acute ischemia but is not the predominant mechanism of chronic scar-related monomorphic VT.
whyWrongByChoice.C: DAD-triggered activity is more associated with digoxin toxicity or catecholamine excess, not scar-related VT in ischemic cardiomyopathy.

questions.stem: A 17-year-old athlete collapses during a football game after being struck in the chest by a ball. He has no prior cardiac history. Bystanders initiate CPR and an AED delivers a shock for ventricular fibrillation. After successful resuscitation, cardiac workup including echocardiography and cardiac MRI is entirely normal. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy

choices.label: B
choices.text: Commotio cordis

choices.label: C
choices.text: Arrhythmogenic right ventricular cardiomyopathy

choices.label: D
choices.text: Long QT syndrome type 1

choices.label: E
choices.text: Brugada syndrome unmasked by exertion

explanation.summary: Commotio cordis is ventricular fibrillation triggered by a blunt, non-penetrating blow to the chest during the vulnerable period of the cardiac cycle (the upslope of the T wave). It occurs in structurally normal hearts and is most common in young athletes hit by projectiles.
explanation.whyCorrect: The combination of chest impact from a projectile, VF arrest in a young athlete, and a completely normal structural cardiac workup is diagnostic of commotio cordis. The impact delivers energy during the vulnerable repolarization window, inducing VF.

whyWrongByChoice.A: HCM would show ventricular hypertrophy on echocardiography and MRI, which are normal here.
whyWrongByChoice.C: ARVC would show RV abnormalities on MRI (fatty/fibrous replacement, wall motion abnormalities), which are absent.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Long QT syndrome would show prolonged QT interval on ECG. Also, the temporal relationship to chest impact, not exertion, points to commotio cordis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Brugada syndrome shows a characteristic ECG pattern (coved ST elevation in V1-V3) that would be detectable. The normal workup and mechanism of chest impact do not support this diagnosis.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old man with Brugada syndrome and a history of syncope undergoes risk stratification. His ECG shows a type 1 Brugada pattern (coved ST elevation ≥2 mm in V1-V2 followed by negative T wave). Which of the following is the recommended treatment to reduce his risk of sudden cardiac death?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Long-term oral amiodarone therapy

choices.label: B
choices.text: Catheter ablation of the right ventricular outflow tract

choices.label: C
choices.text: Implantable cardioverter-defibrillator placement

choices.label: D
choices.text: Long-term oral flecainide therapy

choices.label: E
choices.text: Permanent pacemaker with overdrive pacing

explanation.summary: In Brugada syndrome with spontaneous type 1 ECG pattern and symptoms (syncope or cardiac arrest), an ICD is the recommended treatment for SCD prevention. No antiarrhythmic drug has been proven to reliably prevent VF in Brugada syndrome.
explanation.whyCorrect: ICD implantation is the only proven therapy to prevent SCD in symptomatic Brugada syndrome. The device detects and terminates VF with defibrillation. Syncope with a spontaneous type 1 pattern is a class I indication for ICD.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Amiodarone has not been shown to reliably prevent VF in Brugada syndrome and is not recommended as primary therapy.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Flecainide is a class IC sodium channel blocker that can unmask or worsen the Brugada pattern and is contraindicated.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Pacemaker with overdrive pacing does not prevent VF. While avoiding bradycardia may reduce arrhythmia triggers, pacing alone is insufficient.
whyWrongByChoice.B: While epicardial ablation of the RVOT substrate is emerging as a treatment option, it is not yet the standard of care and is reserved for patients with recurrent ICD shocks.

questions.stem: A 25-year-old woman with congenital long QT syndrome type 2 (KCNH2/hERG mutation) presents after a cardiac arrest triggered by her alarm clock ringing. Which of the following ion channel abnormalities is responsible for QT prolongation in LQT2?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Gain-of-function mutation in the slow sodium channel (SCN5A)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Loss-of-function mutation in the slow delayed rectifier potassium channel (IKs)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Gain-of-function mutation in the L-type calcium channel (CACNA1C)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Loss-of-function mutation in the rapid delayed rectifier potassium channel (IKr)

choices.label: E
choices.text: Gain-of-function mutation in the inward rectifier potassium channel (IK1)

explanation.summary: LQT2 is caused by loss-of-function mutations in KCNH2 (hERG), which encodes the rapid delayed rectifier potassium channel (IKr). Reduced IKr current prolongs phase 3 repolarization, extending the QT interval. Events are typically triggered by auditory stimuli or emotional stress.
explanation.whyCorrect: KCNH2/hERG mutations reduce IKr current, impairing phase 3 repolarization and prolonging the action potential duration (and thus the QT interval). Auditory triggers (alarm clocks, telephones) are characteristic of LQT2 events.

whyWrongByChoice.A: SCN5A gain-of-function mutations cause LQT3, not LQT2. LQT3 involves persistent late sodium current during the plateau phase.
whyWrongByChoice.C: CACNA1C gain-of-function mutations cause Timothy syndrome (LQT8), a rare and severe form of LQTS.
whyWrongByChoice.E: IK1 gain-of-function would shorten the action potential duration (and QT interval), not prolong it.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Loss-of-function in IKs (KCNQ1) causes LQT1, not LQT2. LQT1 events are typically triggered by exercise, especially swimming.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old man with no structural heart disease develops recurrent palpitations. ECG during an episode shows a wide-complex tachycardia with RBBB morphology and left axis deviation. The tachycardia terminates with intravenous verapamil. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ischemic monomorphic ventricular tachycardia

choices.label: B
choices.text: Catecholaminergic polymorphic ventricular tachycardia

choices.label: C
choices.text: Right ventricular outflow tract tachycardia

choices.label: D
choices.text: Bundle branch reentrant ventricular tachycardia

choices.label: E
choices.text: Idiopathic left ventricular fascicular tachycardia (Belhassen VT)

explanation.summary: Idiopathic left ventricular fascicular VT (Belhassen VT) originates from the left posterior fascicle, producing RBBB morphology with left axis deviation. It is characteristically verapamil-sensitive (terminates with IV verapamil), which distinguishes it from other forms of VT.
explanation.whyCorrect: The combination of RBBB morphology, left axis deviation, structurally normal heart, and verapamil sensitivity is pathognomonic for left posterior fascicular VT. This tachycardia uses a reentrant circuit involving the left posterior fascicle and verapamil-sensitive tissue.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Ischemic VT occurs in patients with structural heart disease (prior MI, scar) and does not respond to verapamil.
whyWrongByChoice.C: RVOT VT has LBBB morphology with inferior axis (not RBBB with LAD) and responds to adenosine, not verapamil.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Bundle branch reentry VT occurs in patients with cardiomyopathy and His-Purkinje disease, not structurally normal hearts.
whyWrongByChoice.B: CPVT produces bidirectional VT or polymorphic VT triggered by exercise or catecholamines, not monomorphic VT with RBBB morphology.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with an EF of 30% from nonischemic dilated cardiomyopathy has had no prior arrhythmic events. His medications include sacubitril-valsartan, carvedilol, and spironolactone. He is being evaluated for primary prevention ICD implantation. According to current guidelines, which of the following is the primary indication for ICD in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Nonischemic cardiomyopathy with EF ≤35% despite 3 months of optimal medical therapy

choices.label: B
choices.text: Any heart failure with EF ≤50% regardless of symptoms

choices.label: C
choices.text: Dilated cardiomyopathy with frequent PVCs on Holter monitoring

choices.label: D
choices.text: Heart failure with preserved ejection fraction and syncope

choices.label: E
choices.text: Nonischemic cardiomyopathy with EF ≤45% and nonsustained VT

explanation.summary: Current guidelines recommend ICD for primary prevention of SCD in patients with nonischemic dilated cardiomyopathy and LVEF ≤35% despite at least 3 months of guideline-directed medical therapy (GDMT), based on the SCD-HeFT trial.
explanation.whyCorrect: The SCD-HeFT trial demonstrated mortality benefit from ICD in patients with EF ≤35% (both ischemic and nonischemic) with NYHA class II-III heart failure on optimal medical therapy. The 3-month waiting period allows for potential EF recovery with GDMT.

whyWrongByChoice.B: An EF cutoff of 50% is too high. ICD is indicated at EF ≤35%, not ≤50%, for primary prevention.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Frequent PVCs alone are not an indication for ICD. The EF threshold and heart failure status are the key criteria.
whyWrongByChoice.D: HFpEF (preserved EF) patients are not indicated for primary prevention ICD based on current evidence.
whyWrongByChoice.E: An EF cutoff of 45% is above the guideline threshold of 35%. Additionally, nonsustained VT alone without severely reduced EF is not a primary prevention indication.

questions.stem: A 14-year-old boy with a family history of sudden cardiac death develops palpitations and presyncope during exercise. His resting ECG is normal, but exercise testing provokes bidirectional ventricular tachycardia (alternating QRS axis on consecutive beats). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Long QT syndrome type 1

choices.label: B
choices.text: Catecholaminergic polymorphic ventricular tachycardia (CPVT)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Brugada syndrome

choices.label: D
choices.text: Arrhythmogenic right ventricular cardiomyopathy

choices.label: E
choices.text: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy with outflow obstruction

explanation.summary: CPVT is characterized by exercise-induced bidirectional or polymorphic VT in patients with structurally normal hearts and normal resting ECGs. It is caused by mutations in ryanodine receptor (RyR2) or calsequestrin (CASQ2) genes affecting intracellular calcium handling.
explanation.whyCorrect: Bidirectional VT (alternating QRS axis) triggered by exercise in a young patient with a normal resting ECG and family history of SCD is pathognomonic for CPVT. The arrhythmia results from catecholamine-triggered calcium overload and DADs.

whyWrongByChoice.A: LQT1 can cause exercise-triggered arrhythmias but produces polymorphic VT (torsades de pointes) with QT prolongation on resting ECG, not bidirectional VT with a normal resting ECG.
whyWrongByChoice.D: ARVC produces monomorphic VT with LBBB morphology from RV substrate. It does not characteristically produce bidirectional VT, and structural abnormalities would be visible.
whyWrongByChoice.E: HCM may cause VT during exercise but shows LVH on ECG and echo. The normal resting ECG and bidirectional VT pattern specifically suggest CPVT.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Brugada syndrome produces VF/polymorphic VT typically at rest or during sleep, not exercise-triggered bidirectional VT. The resting ECG would show the Brugada pattern.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with an ICD for secondary prevention of VT experiences an electrical storm (3 appropriate ICD shocks within 24 hours). He is on amiodarone and metoprolol. His potassium is 4.2 mEq/L and magnesium is 2.0 mg/dL. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in acute management?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increase oral amiodarone dose and discharge home

choices.label: B
choices.text: Deactivate the ICD to prevent further shocks

choices.label: C
choices.text: Intravenous lidocaine infusion and sedation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Oral mexiletine loading and outpatient follow-up

choices.label: E
choices.text: Switch amiodarone to oral sotalol

explanation.summary: Electrical storm requires urgent inpatient management with IV antiarrhythmic therapy (lidocaine or IV amiodarone), sedation to reduce sympathetic drive, and correction of any reversible triggers. IV lidocaine is appropriate as adjunctive therapy when VT storm occurs despite oral amiodarone.
explanation.whyCorrect: IV lidocaine provides rapid suppression of VT by blocking sodium channels. Sedation (e.g., with propofol or intubation if needed) reduces catecholamine-driven arrhythmia triggers. This is appropriate acute management for VT storm refractory to oral amiodarone.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Simply increasing oral amiodarone and discharging is inadequate for electrical storm, which requires acute inpatient management with IV therapy.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Oral mexiletine has slow onset and is not appropriate for acute VT storm management. Outpatient follow-up is insufficient for this emergency.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Switching from amiodarone to sotalol acutely is not recommended during electrical storm. Sotalol has proarrhythmic potential and slower onset than IV lidocaine.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Deactivating the ICD removes the life-saving defibrillation capability and is inappropriate unless the patient has a documented do-not-resuscitate order.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old man presents to the emergency department with a wide-complex tachycardia. The Brugada criteria algorithm is applied. Step 1: there is no RS complex in any precordial lead (all concordantly negative). What does this finding indicate according to the Brugada criteria?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The rhythm is SVT with aberrant conduction

choices.label: B
choices.text: The rhythm is atrial flutter with 1:1 conduction

choices.label: C
choices.text: Further criteria steps are needed for differentiation

choices.label: D
choices.text: The rhythm is ventricular tachycardia

choices.label: E
choices.text: The finding is nondiagnostic and clinical context must determine management

explanation.summary: The first step of the Brugada criteria examines whether an RS complex is present in any precordial lead. If no RS complex exists (all concordantly positive or negative), the diagnosis is VT with high specificity. Concordant precordial QRS morphology indicates the depolarization wavefront originates from a single ventricular focus.
explanation.whyCorrect: Absence of an RS complex in all precordial leads (concordant positivity or negativity) is diagnostic of VT by the Brugada criteria. This pattern indicates the electrical wavefront moves uniformly in one direction, consistent with a ventricular origin.

whyWrongByChoice.A: SVT with aberrancy typically shows RS complexes in at least one precordial lead due to sequential bundle branch activation. Concordant negativity favors VT.
whyWrongByChoice.C: When the first step of the Brugada criteria is positive (no RS complex), the diagnosis of VT is made without proceeding to further steps.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The Brugada criteria provide a diagnostic conclusion at this step. The absence of RS complexes is strongly diagnostic of VT.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Atrial flutter with 1:1 conduction would show RS complexes in precordial leads with a bundle branch block pattern, not concordant negativity.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old man has frequent idiopathic PVCs from the right ventricular outflow tract (>20% PVC burden on Holter). He is otherwise healthy with an EF of 50% that was 60% one year ago. He is asymptomatic. Which of the following is the most appropriate management?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Reassurance only, as idiopathic PVCs are always benign

choices.label: B
choices.text: Cardiac transplant evaluation given declining EF

choices.label: C
choices.text: ICD implantation for primary prevention of sudden death

choices.label: D
choices.text: Initiation of oral amiodarone for PVC suppression

choices.label: E
choices.text: Catheter ablation of the PVC focus to prevent cardiomyopathy

explanation.summary: A PVC burden >15-20% can cause PVC-induced cardiomyopathy, characterized by declining EF. This patient's EF has dropped from 60% to 50%, suggesting early cardiomyopathy. Catheter ablation of the RVOT PVC focus eliminates the arrhythmia and typically allows EF recovery.
explanation.whyCorrect: High PVC burden (>15-20%) can cause tachycardia-induced cardiomyopathy with declining EF. Catheter ablation has >90% success for RVOT PVCs and typically results in EF normalization within months if performed before irreversible remodeling occurs.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While many PVCs are benign, a burden >20% with declining EF indicates PVC-induced cardiomyopathy, which requires treatment.
whyWrongByChoice.C: ICD is not indicated here because the cardiomyopathy is potentially reversible with PVC elimination. ICD is for primary prevention in patients with irreversible cardiomyopathy on optimal therapy.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Amiodarone can suppress PVCs but has significant long-term toxicity (thyroid, pulmonary, hepatic). Catheter ablation is preferred for a curable focal arrhythmia.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Cardiac transplant evaluation is premature. The cardiomyopathy is likely reversible with PVC elimination through ablation.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old man is diagnosed with arrhythmogenic right ventricular cardiomyopathy (ARVC). Cardiac MRI shows fatty and fibrous replacement of the RV free wall with RV dilation. He has had one episode of sustained VT with LBBB morphology. Which of the following best explains why VT in ARVC typically has LBBB morphology?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The VT originates from the right ventricle and the wavefront spreads leftward

choices.label: B
choices.text: The VT originates from the left ventricle and conducts rightward

choices.label: C
choices.text: Fibrosis selectively involves the left bundle branch

choices.label: D
choices.text: The accessory pathway in ARVC conducts with LBBB pattern

choices.label: E
choices.text: Both ventricles are simultaneously activated producing a fusion morphology

explanation.summary: VT in ARVC originates from the diseased right ventricle. When the RV depolarizes first, the wavefront spreads from right to left across the septum to the LV, mimicking a left bundle branch block pattern on surface ECG.
explanation.whyCorrect: Ventricular tachycardia arising from the RV produces LBBB morphology because the depolarization wavefront travels from the right ventricle to the left ventricle, the same direction as in native LBBB (where the RV depolarizes first due to left bundle block).

whyWrongByChoice.C: ARVC involves the RV myocardium, not the left bundle branch. The LBBB morphology reflects the RV origin of the tachycardia, not conduction system fibrosis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: ARVC does not involve accessory pathways. The arrhythmias are reentrant through scar tissue in the RV.
whyWrongByChoice.E: VT from a single focus does not simultaneously activate both ventricles. Sequential activation from RV to LV produces the characteristic LBBB pattern.
whyWrongByChoice.B: VT originating from the LV would produce RBBB morphology (leftward-to-rightward spread), not LBBB morphology.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman develops cardiac arrest with pulseless ventricular tachycardia during hospitalization. After 2 minutes of CPR and one defibrillation at 200 J biphasic, the rhythm converts to sinus. Per ACLS protocol, which of the following medications should be administered if VT recurs after the initial successful defibrillation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Intravenous verapamil 5 mg bolus

choices.label: B
choices.text: Intravenous amiodarone 300 mg bolus

choices.label: C
choices.text: Intravenous atropine 1 mg bolus

choices.label: D
choices.text: Intravenous adenosine 12 mg rapid push

choices.label: E
choices.text: Oral sotalol 160 mg loading dose

explanation.summary: Per ACLS guidelines for recurrent pulseless VT/VF, after the first shock and epinephrine, amiodarone 300 mg IV bolus is the recommended antiarrhythmic. A second dose of 150 mg can be given if VT/VF persists.
explanation.whyCorrect: Amiodarone 300 mg IV bolus is the first-line antiarrhythmic in the ACLS algorithm for shock-refractory VT/VF. It has been shown to improve survival to hospital admission compared to placebo or lidocaine.

whyWrongByChoice.A: IV verapamil is contraindicated in pulseless VT because it can cause severe hypotension and cardiovascular collapse in ventricular arrhythmias.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Adenosine is used for stable narrow-complex SVT, not for pulseless VT in cardiac arrest.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Oral medications are not appropriate during cardiac arrest. IV medications are required for rapid onset.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Atropine is used for symptomatic bradycardia, not for VT/VF in cardiac arrest.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man with ischemic cardiomyopathy (EF 20%) and an ICD has been receiving multiple appropriate shocks for recurrent VT despite maximum-dose amiodarone and metoprolol. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Add flecainide to the current antiarrhythmic regimen

choices.label: B
choices.text: Deactivate the ICD and provide comfort care only

choices.label: C
choices.text: VT catheter ablation to eliminate the arrhythmia substrate

choices.label: D
choices.text: Replace the ICD with a newer generation device

choices.label: E
choices.text: Add intravenous digoxin for rate control

explanation.summary: VT catheter ablation targets the scar-related reentrant substrate responsible for recurrent VT. In patients with recurrent ICD shocks despite antiarrhythmic therapy, ablation reduces VT recurrence and ICD shocks, improving quality of life.
explanation.whyCorrect: When VT recurs despite maximal antiarrhythmic therapy, catheter ablation of the scar-related VT substrate is the next step. Ablation can eliminate or reduce the frequency of VT episodes and ICD shocks.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Flecainide is contraindicated in patients with structural heart disease and reduced EF (CAST trial). It would increase mortality risk in this patient.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Replacing the ICD with a newer device does not address the underlying VT substrate. The current device is functioning appropriately.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Digoxin does not treat VT and could cause digoxin toxicity-related arrhythmias. Rate control is not the goal in VT management.
whyWrongByChoice.B: ICD deactivation without the patient's request for comfort care is not appropriate. The goal is to reduce VT burden, not remove life-saving therapy.

questions.stem: A 20-year-old woman with a family history of sudden cardiac death has a resting ECG showing a QTc of 510 ms. She has had no symptoms. Genetic testing reveals a pathogenic SCN5A gain-of-function mutation. Which of the following long QT syndrome types does she have, and what is the characteristic trigger for cardiac events?

choices.label: A
choices.text: LQT1; events triggered by swimming or exertion

choices.label: B
choices.text: LQT2; events triggered by auditory stimuli or emotional stress

choices.label: C
choices.text: LQT5; events triggered by postprandial states

choices.label: D
choices.text: LQT3; events triggered during rest or sleep

choices.label: E
choices.text: LQT7 (Andersen-Tawil); events triggered by hypokalemia

explanation.summary: SCN5A gain-of-function mutations cause LQT3, where persistent late sodium current prolongs repolarization. LQT3 events characteristically occur during rest or sleep (bradycardia-dependent), distinguishing it from LQT1 (exercise) and LQT2 (auditory/emotional triggers).
explanation.whyCorrect: LQT3 is caused by SCN5A gain-of-function, producing a persistent inward sodium current that prolongs the plateau phase. Events occur at rest or during sleep because slower heart rates further prolong the already-extended action potential.

whyWrongByChoice.A: LQT1 is caused by KCNQ1 mutations (IKs loss-of-function) and is triggered by exercise, especially swimming.
whyWrongByChoice.B: LQT2 is caused by KCNH2/hERG mutations (IKr loss-of-function) and is triggered by auditory stimuli and emotional stress.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Andersen-Tawil syndrome (LQT7, KCNJ2 mutations) has periodic paralysis and facial dysmorphism, not solely hypokalemia-triggered events.
whyWrongByChoice.C: LQT5 is caused by KCNE1 mutations and has similar triggers to LQT1, not postprandial events.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man with hypertension, diabetes, and a prior stroke presents with an irregularly irregular pulse at 110 bpm. ECG confirms atrial fibrillation. His CHA₂DS₂-VASc score is calculated. Which of the following is his score?

explanation.summary: CHA₂DS₂-VASc scoring: Congestive HF (0), Hypertension (1), Age ≥75 (2), Diabetes (1), Stroke/TIA (2), Vascular disease (0), Age 65-74 (0, superseded by ≥75), Sex female (0). Total: 0+1+2+1+2+0+0+0 = 6... Actually: age 72 = Age 65-74 gets 1 point (not ≥75). Let me recalculate: HTN=1, Age 65-74=1, DM=1, Stroke=2, Male=0. Total = 5.
explanation.whyCorrect: CHA₂DS₂-VASc: C=0, H=1 (hypertension), A₂=0 (age 72, not ≥75), D=1 (diabetes), S₂=2 (prior stroke), V=0, A=1 (age 65-74), Sc=0 (male). Total = 1+0+1+2+0+1+0 = 5.

whyWrongByChoice.A: A score of 2 underestimates the risk by not accounting for all comorbidities, particularly the high-weight stroke history.
whyWrongByChoice.B: A score of 3 fails to include all applicable risk factors.
whyWrongByChoice.C: A score of 4 likely miscounts the stroke component (which receives 2 points, not 1).
whyWrongByChoice.D: A score of 6 overcounts, likely by adding age ≥75 (2 points) instead of age 65-74 (1 point), or adding a condition not present.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old woman with persistent atrial fibrillation is undergoing evaluation for rhythm control. Her left atrial diameter is 5.8 cm on echocardiography. She has had AF for 3 years. Which of the following pathologic changes in the atrial myocardium most contributes to the perpetuation of AF in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Atrial fibrosis with electrical remodeling and shortened refractory periods

choices.label: B
choices.text: Atrial myocyte hypertrophy with preserved gap junctions

choices.label: C
choices.text: Increased atrial myocyte automaticity from sodium channel upregulation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Decreased atrial size reducing wavelength for reentry

choices.label: E
choices.text: Enhanced parasympathetic innervation prolonging atrial refractoriness

explanation.summary: Long-standing AF causes structural remodeling (fibrosis) and electrical remodeling (shortened atrial refractory periods due to calcium channel downregulation). These changes create more favorable conditions for multiple reentrant wavelets to sustain AF ('AF begets AF').
explanation.whyCorrect: Atrial fibrosis creates heterogeneous conduction with areas of slow conduction and block. Electrical remodeling shortens the atrial effective refractory period, decreasing the wavelength for reentry. Both promote more and smaller reentrant circuits, perpetuating AF.

whyWrongByChoice.C: AF is not primarily driven by enhanced automaticity from sodium channels. The main mechanism is multiple reentrant wavelets sustained by structural and electrical remodeling.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Atrial dilation (increased size, not decreased) is present here (5.8 cm). Larger atria can support more reentrant wavelets, not fewer.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Parasympathetic stimulation shortens (not prolongs) atrial refractoriness, which can trigger AF but is not the primary mechanism of chronic AF perpetuation.
whyWrongByChoice.B: AF causes gap junction remodeling (disorganization and downregulation), not preservation. Myocyte hypertrophy occurs but gap junction dysfunction contributes to conduction abnormalities.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man with paroxysmal atrial fibrillation is referred for catheter ablation. The electrophysiologist plans to isolate the pulmonary veins. Which of the following best explains why the pulmonary veins are the primary ablation target in paroxysmal AF?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The pulmonary veins contain the AV node which initiates AF

choices.label: B
choices.text: Rapidly firing ectopic foci in pulmonary vein sleeves trigger AF episodes

choices.label: C
choices.text: The pulmonary veins form the critical isthmus for the AF reentrant circuit

choices.label: D
choices.text: Pulmonary vein stenosis from inflammation causes atrial pressure overload

choices.label: E
choices.text: The pulmonary veins contain sympathetic ganglia that drive AF

explanation.summary: The seminal discovery by Haïssaguerre et al. (1998) showed that rapidly firing ectopic foci within the muscular sleeves of the pulmonary veins are the primary triggers for paroxysmal AF. Electrical isolation of the PVs from the left atrium eliminates these triggers.
explanation.whyCorrect: Pulmonary vein myocardial sleeves contain cells capable of rapid, repetitive firing that triggers AF. Circumferential ablation around the PV ostia creates electrical isolation, preventing these triggers from reaching the left atrium and initiating AF.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The AV node is located in the triangle of Koch in the right atrium, not in the pulmonary veins. The AV node does not initiate AF.
whyWrongByChoice.C: AF in the paroxysmal phase is typically triggered, not sustained by a single reentrant circuit through the PVs. PVs are triggers, not an obligatory circuit isthmus.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Ablation does not treat PV stenosis. In fact, PV stenosis is a potential complication of ablation, not a cause of AF.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While autonomic ganglia near the PVs may modulate AF, the primary mechanism is ectopic firing from the PV muscular sleeves, not ganglionic activity.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man with nonvalvular atrial fibrillation and a CHA₂DS₂-VASc score of 4 is started on apixaban for stroke prevention. Which of the following best describes the mechanism of action of apixaban?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Direct thrombin (factor IIa) inhibitor

choices.label: B
choices.text: Vitamin K epoxide reductase inhibitor

choices.label: C
choices.text: Direct factor Xa inhibitor

choices.label: D
choices.text: Antithrombin III activator (indirect factor Xa inhibition)

choices.label: E
choices.text: Glycoprotein IIb/IIIa receptor antagonist

explanation.summary: Apixaban is a direct oral anticoagulant (DOAC) that directly and selectively inhibits factor Xa, a key enzyme in the common pathway of the coagulation cascade. It does not require antithrombin III as a cofactor.
explanation.whyCorrect: Apixaban directly binds to and inhibits factor Xa (both free and prothrombinase-bound), preventing the conversion of prothrombin to thrombin. This reduces thrombin generation and subsequent fibrin clot formation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Direct thrombin inhibition is the mechanism of dabigatran, not apixaban.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Indirect factor Xa inhibition via antithrombin III activation describes the mechanism of heparin and fondaparinux, not apixaban.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Glycoprotein IIb/IIIa antagonism is the mechanism of abciximab, eptifibatide, and tirofiban (antiplatelet agents), not apixaban.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Vitamin K epoxide reductase inhibition is the mechanism of warfarin, not apixaban.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old woman with persistent AF undergoes elective cardioversion. She has been on therapeutic anticoagulation with rivaroxaban for 4 weeks. After successful cardioversion to sinus rhythm, how long should anticoagulation be continued regardless of her CHA₂DS₂-VASc score?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Anticoagulation can be stopped immediately after cardioversion

choices.label: B
choices.text: At least 1 week post-cardioversion

choices.label: C
choices.text: At least 3 months post-cardioversion

choices.label: D
choices.text: At least 4 weeks post-cardioversion

choices.label: E
choices.text: At least 6 months post-cardioversion

explanation.summary: After cardioversion for AF lasting >48 hours, anticoagulation must be continued for at least 4 weeks post-cardioversion, regardless of CHA₂DS₂-VASc score. This is because atrial mechanical function may take weeks to recover ('atrial stunning'), leaving the patient at risk for thrombus formation.
explanation.whyCorrect: Post-cardioversion atrial stunning can persist for up to 4 weeks, during which thrombus may form despite restoration of sinus rhythm. Therefore, 4 weeks of post-cardioversion anticoagulation is mandatory regardless of stroke risk score.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Immediate discontinuation places the patient at high risk of stroke due to atrial stunning and delayed mechanical recovery.
whyWrongByChoice.B: One week is insufficient because atrial mechanical recovery may take 2-4 weeks, leaving the patient at continued risk.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Six months is not a standard guideline recommendation. The minimum is 4 weeks; longer anticoagulation depends on individual risk assessment.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Three months exceeds the minimum recommendation. Long-term anticoagulation decisions beyond 4 weeks are based on the CHA₂DS₂-VASc score.

questions.stem: A 75-year-old man with permanent atrial fibrillation has a resting ventricular rate of 115 bpm despite metoprolol 100 mg twice daily. He has heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (EF 35%). Which of the following medications would be most appropriate to add for rate control?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Verapamil 120 mg three times daily

choices.label: B
choices.text: Flecainide 100 mg twice daily

choices.label: C
choices.text: Dronedarone 400 mg twice daily

choices.label: D
choices.text: Diltiazem 180 mg extended-release daily

choices.label: E
choices.text: Digoxin 0.125 mg daily

explanation.summary: In HFrEF with inadequate rate control on a beta-blocker, digoxin is the appropriate add-on rate control agent. Non-dihydropyridine calcium channel blockers (verapamil, diltiazem) are contraindicated in HFrEF due to negative inotropy. Dronedarone is contraindicated in permanent AF.
explanation.whyCorrect: Digoxin slows AV nodal conduction via vagal enhancement and is safe in HFrEF (no negative inotropy). It is the appropriate add-on to beta-blockers for rate control in patients with reduced EF.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Verapamil has significant negative inotropic effects and is contraindicated in HFrEF. It can worsen heart failure and cause hemodynamic collapse.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Flecainide is a rhythm control (not rate control) agent and is contraindicated in structural heart disease and reduced EF (CAST trial).
whyWrongByChoice.D: Diltiazem, like verapamil, has negative inotropic effects and is contraindicated in HFrEF.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Dronedarone is specifically contraindicated in permanent AF and in severe heart failure (ANDROMEDA trial showed increased mortality in NYHA III-IV HF).

questions.stem: A 62-year-old man with paroxysmal AF and no structural heart disease is started on flecainide for rhythm control. His physician also prescribes a low-dose beta-blocker or calcium channel blocker to be taken with flecainide. Which of the following best explains the reason for this combination?

choices.label: A
choices.text: To prevent 1:1 AV conduction of atrial flutter that may be organized by flecainide

choices.label: B
choices.text: To enhance the efficacy of flecainide by prolonging the QT interval

choices.label: C
choices.text: To counteract the positive inotropic effects of flecainide

choices.label: D
choices.text: To reduce the hepatic metabolism of flecainide

choices.label: E
choices.text: To prevent flecainide-induced QT prolongation and torsades de pointes

explanation.summary: Flecainide can convert AF to atrial flutter with a slower atrial rate (e.g., 200 bpm instead of 300 bpm). This slower rate may allow 1:1 AV conduction, causing dangerously rapid ventricular rates. An AV nodal blocking agent prevents this potentially life-threatening complication.
explanation.whyCorrect: Class IC drugs can organize the chaotic atrial activity of AF into organized atrial flutter with a slower atrial rate that the AV node can conduct 1:1. A concurrent AV nodal blocker ensures the ventricular rate remains controlled even if this occurs.

whyWrongByChoice.C: Flecainide has negative inotropic effects, not positive ones. The beta-blocker is not added to counteract inotropy.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Beta-blockers and calcium channel blockers do not significantly alter flecainide metabolism. The combination is for pharmacodynamic, not pharmacokinetic, reasons.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Flecainide does not cause significant QT prolongation or torsades de pointes. Its proarrhythmic risk is related to sodium channel blockade and 1:1 flutter conduction.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Flecainide primarily affects the QRS duration (sodium channels), not the QT interval. The combination is not meant to prolong QT.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with mitral stenosis from rheumatic heart disease develops atrial fibrillation. His cardiologist recommends anticoagulation with warfarin rather than a DOAC. Which of the following best explains why warfarin is preferred over DOACs in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: DOACs are less effective at preventing stroke in any form of AF

choices.label: B
choices.text: Valvular AF with moderate-severe mitral stenosis was excluded from DOAC trials

choices.label: C
choices.text: DOACs cannot be reversed in case of major bleeding

choices.label: D
choices.text: Warfarin has fewer drug interactions than DOACs

choices.label: E
choices.text: DOACs require more frequent monitoring than warfarin

explanation.summary: Patients with moderate-to-severe mitral stenosis (and mechanical heart valves) were excluded from the landmark DOAC trials (RE-LY, ROCKET-AF, ARISTOTLE, ENGAGE-AF). Without evidence of safety and efficacy in this population, warfarin remains the recommended anticoagulant.
explanation.whyCorrect: The major DOAC trials excluded patients with hemodynamically significant mitral stenosis. Since there is no evidence demonstrating DOAC safety and efficacy in this 'valvular AF' population, warfarin with INR monitoring remains standard of care.

whyWrongByChoice.A: DOACs are at least as effective as warfarin for stroke prevention in nonvalvular AF. The issue is lack of evidence in valvular AF, not inferior efficacy.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Reversal agents exist for DOACs: idarucizumab for dabigatran and andexanet alfa for factor Xa inhibitors. Irreversibility is not the reason.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Warfarin has far more drug and food interactions than DOACs. This is actually a disadvantage of warfarin.
whyWrongByChoice.E: DOACs require less monitoring than warfarin. Warfarin requires regular INR monitoring, while DOACs do not.

questions.stem: A 66-year-old man with longstanding persistent AF has a left atrial appendage thrombus discovered on transesophageal echocardiography. He was scheduled for cardioversion today. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Proceed with cardioversion and start heparin simultaneously

choices.label: B
choices.text: Proceed with cardioversion using higher energy (360 J monophasic)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Cancel cardioversion, anticoagulate for 3-4 weeks, then repeat TEE

choices.label: D
choices.text: Perform surgical thrombectomy before cardioversion

choices.label: E
choices.text: Start aspirin and clopidogrel dual antiplatelet therapy instead

explanation.summary: When a left atrial appendage thrombus is found on TEE, cardioversion must be postponed because cardioversion with a thrombus present carries a high risk of systemic embolization (stroke). The patient should be anticoagulated for at least 3-4 weeks to allow thrombus resolution, then a repeat TEE should confirm thrombus resolution before attempting cardioversion.
explanation.whyCorrect: Cardioversion in the presence of atrial thrombus can dislodge the clot causing stroke or systemic embolism. The standard approach is to defer cardioversion, anticoagulate adequately, and repeat TEE to confirm thrombus resolution before proceeding.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Proceeding with cardioversion despite known thrombus is dangerous and contraindicated due to embolization risk.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Surgical thrombectomy is overly invasive for this situation. Adequate anticoagulation typically resolves the thrombus without surgery.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Dual antiplatelet therapy is inferior to anticoagulation for thrombus resolution and stroke prevention in AF.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Higher energy does not address the thrombus risk. The concern is embolization, not the energy level of cardioversion.

questions.stem: A 48-year-old man with lone paroxysmal AF (no structural heart disease, no hypertension, no diabetes) has a CHA₂DS₂-VASc score of 0. He has AF episodes every 2-3 months lasting 4-6 hours. Which of the following is the most appropriate approach to anticoagulation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Long-term oral anticoagulation with apixaban

choices.label: B
choices.text: Aspirin 325 mg daily for stroke prevention

choices.label: C
choices.text: Anticoagulation only during AF episodes

choices.label: D
choices.text: No anticoagulation or antiplatelet therapy recommended

choices.label: E
choices.text: Clopidogrel 75 mg daily for stroke prevention

explanation.summary: For men with a CHA₂DS₂-VASc score of 0, the stroke risk is low enough that neither anticoagulation nor antiplatelet therapy is recommended. Aspirin is no longer recommended for stroke prevention in AF due to insufficient efficacy and bleeding risk.
explanation.whyCorrect: Current guidelines state that anticoagulation is not recommended for males with CHA₂DS₂-VASc 0 (or females with score 1, where the only point is female sex). The annual stroke risk is <1%, and the bleeding risk of anticoagulation outweighs the benefit.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Anticoagulation is not recommended for CHA₂DS₂-VASc 0 in males because the stroke risk is very low and does not outweigh the bleeding risk of DOACs.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Aspirin is no longer recommended for stroke prevention in AF. It has been shown to be ineffective and still carries bleeding risk.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Clopidogrel alone is not an established therapy for stroke prevention in AF.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Intermittent anticoagulation during AF episodes is not recommended because stroke risk exists before, during, and after AF episodes, and this approach is impractical and unproven.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with new-onset atrial fibrillation presents to the emergency department within 12 hours of symptom onset. He is hemodynamically stable with a ventricular rate of 140 bpm. TEE shows no left atrial thrombus. The emergency physician decides to attempt cardioversion. Which of the following is a prerequisite before cardioversion in this scenario?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Initiation of amiodarone loading for at least 24 hours

choices.label: B
choices.text: Therapeutic anticoagulation for at least 3 weeks prior

choices.label: C
choices.text: Correction of potassium level to above 5.0 mEq/L

choices.label: D
choices.text: Cardiology consultation for catheter ablation

choices.label: E
choices.text: Administration of anticoagulation (e.g., heparin) before cardioversion

explanation.summary: When AF duration is <48 hours (or TEE confirms no thrombus), cardioversion can proceed without 3 weeks of prior anticoagulation. However, anticoagulation (typically IV heparin) should be administered before or at the time of cardioversion to prevent thrombus formation during the procedure.
explanation.whyCorrect: Even when AF is <48 hours old or TEE excludes thrombus, periprocedural anticoagulation is necessary because cardioversion itself can promote thrombus formation from atrial stunning. Heparin is given before cardioversion, and anticoagulation is continued for at least 4 weeks afterward.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Amiodarone loading is optional for pharmacologic cardioversion but is not a prerequisite for electrical cardioversion.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Three weeks of anticoagulation is required when AF duration is >48 hours and TEE is not performed. Since this patient's AF is <48 hours and TEE is negative, 3 weeks is not needed.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Catheter ablation is not a prerequisite for cardioversion. It is considered for recurrent AF after failed medical therapy.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Potassium should be maintained in the normal range (3.5-5.0 mEq/L). Targeting above 5.0 would cause hyperkalemia, which is dangerous.

questions.stem: A 73-year-old woman with AF, hypertension, diabetes, and chronic kidney disease (CrCl 22 mL/min) needs anticoagulation for stroke prevention. Her CHA₂DS₂-VASc score is 5. Which of the following is the most appropriate anticoagulant choice?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Apixaban 2.5 mg twice daily

choices.label: B
choices.text: Dabigatran 150 mg twice daily

choices.label: C
choices.text: Rivaroxaban 20 mg daily

choices.label: D
choices.text: Edoxaban 60 mg daily

choices.label: E
choices.text: No anticoagulation due to high bleeding risk

explanation.summary: In patients with severe CKD (CrCl 15-25 mL/min), apixaban at the reduced dose of 2.5 mg twice daily is the preferred DOAC. It has the least renal clearance among DOACs (~27%) and has been studied in patients with advanced CKD. Dabigatran is contraindicated in severe CKD (CrCl <30 mL/min).
explanation.whyCorrect: Apixaban has the lowest renal dependence of all DOACs and is approved for use down to CrCl 15 mL/min (and even in dialysis patients per FDA labeling). The dose-reduced 2.5 mg BID is appropriate for this patient with CrCl 22 mL/min.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Dabigatran is approximately 80% renally cleared and is contraindicated when CrCl <30 mL/min due to accumulation and bleeding risk.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Edoxaban requires CrCl 15-95 mL/min and would need dose reduction. Apixaban is generally preferred in severe CKD due to lower renal clearance.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Withholding anticoagulation with a CHA₂DS₂-VASc of 5 places the patient at very high stroke risk. The benefit of anticoagulation outweighs bleeding risk, and apixaban can be safely used.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Rivaroxaban 20 mg is the full dose; even the reduced dose (15 mg) requires CrCl ≥15-50 mL/min. However, rivaroxaban has higher renal clearance (~36%) than apixaban, making apixaban preferred.

questions.stem: A 67-year-old man on warfarin for atrial fibrillation presents with hematemesis. His INR is 8.5. He is hemodynamically stable with hemoglobin of 9.2 g/dL. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial management to reverse the coagulopathy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Vitamin K 10 mg IV and fresh frozen plasma

choices.label: B
choices.text: 4-factor prothrombin complex concentrate (4F-PCC) and IV vitamin K

choices.label: C
choices.text: Oral vitamin K 5 mg alone and hold warfarin

choices.label: D
choices.text: Recombinant factor VIIa 90 mcg/kg IV

choices.label: E
choices.text: Tranexamic acid 1 g IV and cryoprecipitate

explanation.summary: For major bleeding on warfarin with significantly elevated INR, 4-factor PCC provides rapid, complete reversal of warfarin's anticoagulant effect by directly replacing vitamin K-dependent clotting factors (II, VII, IX, X). IV vitamin K (10 mg) is given concurrently for sustained reversal as PCC effects wane.
explanation.whyCorrect: 4F-PCC is the preferred agent for emergent warfarin reversal in major bleeding because it rapidly restores coagulation factor levels. IV vitamin K provides sustained reversal (onset 6-12 hours) to prevent re-anticoagulation as PCC effects dissipate.

whyWrongByChoice.A: FFP was historically used but is inferior to 4F-PCC: it requires large volumes (risk of volume overload), takes longer to prepare and administer, and achieves less complete INR correction.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Oral vitamin K alone is appropriate for asymptomatic INR elevation without bleeding, not for active major hemorrhage requiring urgent reversal.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Recombinant factor VIIa is not recommended for warfarin reversal due to high cost, thrombotic complications, and incomplete correction of all vitamin K-dependent factors.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Tranexamic acid is an antifibrinolytic and cryoprecipitate replaces fibrinogen. Neither addresses the vitamin K-dependent factor deficiency caused by warfarin.

questions.stem: A 78-year-old woman with permanent AF and HFrEF (EF 25%) has a resting ventricular rate of 130 bpm despite maximal doses of metoprolol and digoxin. She remains symptomatic with dyspnea and fatigue. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Add oral amiodarone for rhythm control

choices.label: B
choices.text: Pulmonary vein isolation catheter ablation

choices.label: C
choices.text: AV node ablation with biventricular pacemaker implantation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Add oral dronedarone for rate and rhythm control

choices.label: E
choices.text: Increase digoxin dose to achieve a level of 3.0 ng/mL

explanation.summary: In patients with permanent AF, HFrEF, and refractory rapid ventricular rates despite medical therapy, AV node ablation with biventricular (CRT) pacemaker implantation ('ablate and pace') achieves definitive rate control and provides cardiac resynchronization to improve EF and symptoms.
explanation.whyCorrect: AV node ablation eliminates the rapid ventricular response by creating complete heart block, and a biventricular pacemaker provides physiologic ventricular activation. This strategy improves symptoms, exercise capacity, and EF in patients with refractory rates.

whyWrongByChoice.A: In permanent AF, rhythm control is not pursued (by definition, permanent AF is accepted). Additionally, amiodarone has significant long-term toxicity.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Dronedarone is contraindicated in permanent AF (increased cardiovascular events in PALLAS trial) and in NYHA III-IV heart failure (increased mortality in ANDROMEDA trial).
whyWrongByChoice.E: Targeting a digoxin level of 3.0 ng/mL is dangerous. Therapeutic range is 0.5-2.0 ng/mL; levels above 2.0 cause toxicity including fatal arrhythmias.
whyWrongByChoice.B: PVI is a rhythm control strategy inappropriate for permanent AF (where rhythm control has been abandoned). It is more effective for paroxysmal AF.
